topics.title: Test on stress-strain characteristics of mild steel and Tor Steel by conducting uniaxial tension test on rod specimens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Test on stress-strain characteristics of mild steel and Tor Steel by conducting uniaxial tension test on rod specimens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Uniaxial Tension Test** (also called the Tensile Test) is a fundamental **destructive mechanical test** conducted on ductile metallic materials like steel to evaluate their mechanical and elastic-plastic behaviour under a gradually increasing axial pulling load. The test is governed by **IS 1608:2005** (reaffirmed 2021) for metallic materials and **IS 432:1982** / **IS 1786:2008** for the specimen grades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCVL307 | Module 2 Experiment):**
> The student is expected to perform the uniaxial tension test on a **mild steel (MS) round rod** and a **Tor Steel (HYSD bar)** specimen, plot the **load vs elongation curve**, convert it to the **stress–strain curve**, and report the following mechanical properties:
> 1. Young's Modulus (E)
> 2. Upper and Lower Yield Stress (σ_yu, σ_yl)
> 3. Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u)
> 4. Percentage Elongation (%El)
> 5. Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA)

## 1.2 Material Definitions

| Term | Definition | Key Identifier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mild Steel (MS)** | Low-carbon steel (C = 0.15 % to 0.25 %) with **plain smooth surface**, conforming to **IS 432** grade Fe 250. | Plain bar, distinct yield point |
| **Tor Steel (HYSD)** | High Yield Strength Deformed steel bar with **transverse ribs/lugs**, conforming to **IS 1786** grades Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550. | Deformed surface, no distinct yield |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Taffy Pull"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pulling a piece of warm taffy (jaggery-mixture) with both hands.
> 1. **Initial Stage:** It resists stiffly — you can feel it springing back if released (this is the **Elastic Region**).
> 2. **Sudden Stretches:** It suddenly gives way and stretches with almost no extra force (this is the **Yield Plateau**).
> 3. **Strain Hardening:** As you keep pulling, the stretched part becomes harder and resists again (this is **Strain Hardening**).
> 4. **Thinning & Snapping:** The middle becomes very thin (**Necking**) and finally snaps (**Fracture**).
> 
> The mild steel specimen behaves EXACTLY like this taffy. Tor Steel is a "stubborn cousin" — it refuses to give a sudden yield plateau, so engineers define its yield by a 0.2 % proof stress offset.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standards

- **Young's Modulus of Steel (E) = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)**
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν) = 0.30**
- **Density of Steel = 7850 kg/m³**
- **Standard Gauge Length Ratio (k) = 5.65√A₀** (proportional specimen per IS 1608)
- **Standard Codes:** IS 432, IS 1608, IS 1786, IS 2062
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — The Stress-Strain Behaviour

The uniaxial tension test progressively stretches a cylindrical rod specimen along its longitudinal axis. The **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** records the **load (P)** and the corresponding **elongation (ΔL)** continuously. These raw data are mathematically normalised to obtain **engineering stress (σ)** and **engineering strain (ε)**.

### 2.1.1 Stages of the Mild Steel Stress-Strain Curve (The "Master Key" Topic)

The curve of mild steel exhibits **four distinct regions** which the examiner repeatedly tests:

**Stage 1 — Linear Elastic Region (OA)**
- Stress is **directly proportional** to strain (Hooke's Law holds).
- The slope gives **Young's Modulus E = σ/ε**.
- If the load is removed, the specimen returns to its original length completely.

**Stage 2 — Yield Region (B to D)**
- **Point B** = Upper Yield Point (σ_yu) — the stress at which plastic deformation first begins, marked by a sudden drop.
- **Point C** = Lower Yield Point (σ_yl) — the steady stress at which the specimen yields continuously.
- A nearly **horizontal plateau** appears where the specimen elongates without any increase in load.

**Stage 3 — Strain-Hardening Region (D to G)**
- Beyond yielding, the material's internal crystalline structure reorganises and becomes **stronger** with deformation.
- The curve rises again up to the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u) at Point G**.

**Stage 4 — Necking and Fracture (G to I)**
- Localised reduction in cross-section (called **necking**) begins at the weakest cross-section.
- Engineering stress computed from the **original area A₀** appears to drop, even though the *true* stress continues to rise.
- The specimen finally **fractures at Point I**.

### 2.1.2 Behaviour of Tor Steel (HYSD Bar) — The Critical Difference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Tor Steel does **NOT** exhibit a clear yield plateau because of its high carbon content and surface deformations. Therefore, **Indian Standard IS 1786** specifies the use of the **0.2 % Proof Stress (σ_0.2)** as the operational yield strength.
> 
> **Definition:** 0.2 % proof stress is the stress at which the unloading line, drawn parallel to the initial elastic slope from a strain of 0.2 %, intersects the stress-strain curve.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | A₀ is the **original** cross-sectional area |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless | L₀ is the original gauge length |
| 3 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | N/mm² or GPa | Slope of linear elastic region |
| 4 | Yield Stress | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{P_y}{A_0}$ | N/mm² | P_y = load at yield point |
| 5 | Ultimate Stress | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | N/mm² | P_max = maximum load recorded |
| 6 | % Elongation | $\% El = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Indicator of **ductility** |
| 7 | % Reduction in Area | $\% RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_f} \times 100$ | % | Another ductility measure |
| 8 | Gauge Length (proportional) | $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ | mm | Per IS 1608, k = 5.65 |
| 9 | Cross-section area of rod | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | mm² | d = original diameter |
| 10 | 0.2 % Proof Stress | $\sigma_{0.2} = \dfrac{P_{0.2}}{A_0}$ | N/mm² | Operational yield for Tor Steel |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Civil Engineering

- **Design of RC Structures:** Yield stress decides the working strength of the bar; IS 456:2000 limits the design strength to **0.87 × f_y** to account for over-strength.
- **Quality Control at Site:** Tor Steel rebars are routinely tested in site labs to confirm the manufacturer's mill certificate.
- **Failure Analysis:** The % elongation and fracture surface (cup-and-cone) help identify whether a bar failed due to overload, brittle defect, or corrosion.
- **Weldability & Bending Tests:** The ductility data govern whether a bar can be bent or welded on site.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stress, Strain and Modulus

### 3.1.1 Engineering Stress

By Newton's second law and the definition of stress as force per unit area, when an axial load **P** acts on a rod of original cross-sectional area **A₀**, the internal resisting force at any cross-section equals **P**. Hence:

$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{Resisting Force}}{\text{Original Area}} = \frac{P}{A_0}
$$

> **Why original area?** Engineering stress is intentionally computed with the *initial* area so that results from different specimens (with different diameters) can be compared on a common normalised scale. True stress, which uses the instantaneous area, is reserved for advanced metal-forming studies.

### 3.1.2 Engineering Strain

Strain is the **change in length per unit original length**:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}
$$

where L is the current length and L₀ is the initial gauge length. The value is dimensionless and is often reported as a percentage or in micro-strain (10⁻⁶) in advanced instrumentation.

### 3.1.3 Young's Modulus

Within the proportional (linear elastic) limit, Hooke's Law states σ = E · ε. Rearranging:

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P \, L_0}{A_0 \, \Delta L}
$$

For standard reinforcing steel, **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** is the design value mandated by IS 456.

## 3.2 Sample Analytical Walkthrough (KTU Board-Standard)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A mild steel rod specimen of **diameter d = 20 mm** and **gauge length L₀ = 200 mm** is tested in a UTM. The **load at the upper yield point = 78 kN**, **load at the lower yield point = 70 kN**, and the **maximum (ultimate) load = 118 kN**. After fracture, the **final gauge length L_f = 240 mm** and **final diameter d_f = 14 mm**. Calculate the mechanical properties.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the original cross-sectional area**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the upper and lower yield stresses**

$$
\sigma_{yu} = \frac{P_{yu}}{A_0} = \frac{78\,000}{314.16} = 248.3 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{yl} = \frac{P_{yl}}{A_0} = \frac{70\,000}{314.16} = 222.8 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ultimate tensile strength**

$$
\sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{118\,000}{314.16} = 375.6 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage elongation**

$$
\% El = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{240 - 200}{200} \times 100 = 20.00 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute percentage reduction in area**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (14)^2 = 153.94 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\% RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 153.94}{314.16} \times 100 = 51.00 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Result Summary (Analytical Matrix)**

| Property | Symbol | Value | Standard Reference |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Upper Yield Stress | σ_yu | **248.3 MPa** | IS 432 expects ≥ 250 MPa |
| Lower Yield Stress | σ_yl | **222.8 MPa** | — |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | σ_u | **375.6 MPa** | IS 432 expects 410–530 MPa |
| Percentage Elongation | %El | **20.00 %** | IS 432 expects ≥ 23 % |
| Percentage Reduction in Area | %RA | **51.00 %** | Indicates good ductility |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marking Trap #1:**
> A common mistake is to use the **final** diameter d_f in computing the yield and ultimate stresses. Always remember: **engineering stress uses A₀ (original area)**. Using A_f will inflate the calculated strength and is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

## 3.3 Worked Example for Tor Steel (0.2 % Proof Stress)

> A Tor Steel bar of **diameter 16 mm** is tested. The **0.2 % proof load = 95 kN** and the **ultimate load = 130 kN**. Find σ_0.2 and σ_u.

**Step 1 — Area**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — 0.2 % Proof Stress**

$$
\sigma_{0.2} = \frac{95\,000}{201.06} = 472.5 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate Stress**

$$
\sigma_u = \frac{130\,000}{201.06} = 646.5 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

> This bar conforms to **IS 1786 Fe 500 grade** (σ_0.2 = 500 MPa, σ_u ≥ 545 MPa) — *marginally* below the standard. Hence it is **rejected for site use**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following flowchart maps the **load path** and the **data flow** of a 1000 kN capacity UTM (the standard machine available in KTU-affiliated civil engineering labs).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    op[Operator Console] --> pump[Hydraulic Power Pack]
    pump --> cyl[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    cyl --> cross[Moving Crosshead]
    cross --> lg[Lower Grip Assembly]
    lg --> spec[Steel Rod Specimen]
    spec --> ug[Upper Grip Assembly]
    ug --> lcell[Load Cell]
    lcell --> frame[Fixed Upper Crosshead]
    frame --> col[Loading Frame Columns]
    ext[Extensometer / Dial Gauge] --> spec
    lcell --> daq[Data Acquisition Card]
    ext --> daq
    daq --> mon[PC Monitor with X-Y Plotter]
    daq --> printer[Thermal Printer for Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows in the upper half depict the **mechanical load path** (from operator pressing 'Start' all the way to specimen fracture). Arrows in the lower half depict the **data flow** (analog signals from load cell and extensometer are digitised and plotted as Load vs Elongation, then converted to Stress vs Strain).

## 4.2 Step-by-Step Test Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s([START]) --> p1[Step 1: Prepare Specimen per IS 1608]
    p1 --> p2[Step 2: Measure d0, L0 with Vernier and Scale]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Mark Gauge Length with Punch Marks]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: Mount Specimen in UTM Grips]
    p4 --> p5[Step 5: Align Specimen Axially to Avoid Bending]
    p5 --> p6[Step 6: Attach Extensometer on Gauge Length]
    p6 --> p7[Step 7: Zero Load and Extension Readings]
    p7 --> p8[Step 8: Apply Load at Constant Rate 10 MPa per sec]
    p8 --> p9[Step 9: Record Yield Load Py and Ultimate Load Pu]
    p9 --> p10[Step 10: Continue Loading Until Fracture]
    p10 --> p11[Step 11: Carefully Remove Fractured Pieces]
    p11 --> p12[Step 12: Measure Lf and df at Fracture]
    p12 --> p13[Step 13: Calculate Properties and Plot Curve]
    p13 --> e([END])
```

## 4.3 Stress-Strain Curve — Key Points and Regions (Mild Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin O 0 0] --> A[Point A Proportional Limit sigma equals 200 MPa]
    A --> B[Point B Elastic Limit]
    B --> C[Point C Upper Yield Point sigma_yu]
    C --> D[Point D Lower Yield Point sigma_yl]
    D --> E[Region E to F Yield Plateau]
    E --> F[Point F Onset of Strain Hardening]
    F --> G[Point G Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma_u]
    G --> H[Region H Necking Begins]
    H --> I[Point I Fracture Point sigma_f]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram for the exam:** The horizontal flow of points mirrors the actual stress-strain curve. Memorise the **sequence O → A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → I** as a story. Examiners frequently ask: *"Why does the curve drop after G even though the material is not weakening?"* The correct answer is **engineering stress uses original area A₀, but the area reduces rapidly during necking, making σ = P/A₀ appear to drop**.

## 4.4 Specimen Geometry — Before and After Testing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Before["BEFORE TEST Uniform Cylindrical Rod"]
        b1[Original Diameter d0]
        b2[Gauge Length L0 between Punch Marks]
        b3[Original Cross Section A0]
    end
    subgraph After["AFTER TEST Necked and Fractured"]
        a1[Final Diameter df at Neck]
        a2[Final Gauge Length Lf]
        a3[Reduced Cross Section Af]
        a4[Cup and Cone Fracture Surface]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **cup-and-cone fracture** is a signature of **ductile failure**. The central flat region is where micro-voids coalesced; the outer slant is where shear lip formed. A **flat, brittle fracture** (with no necking) would indicate a problem with the steel such as excessive carbon or hydrogen embrittlement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Q1, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q1.** Define the **Uniaxial Tension Test**. List **any four** mechanical properties of steel that are determined from this test.

**Model Answer:**
The uniaxial tension test is a standard destructive mechanical test in which a prepared cylindrical rod specimen is subjected to a gradually increasing axial tensile load on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) until it fractures. The load and corresponding elongation are recorded continuously to plot a stress-strain curve.
The four key mechanical properties determined are:
1. **Young's Modulus (E)** — from the slope of the linear elastic region.
2. **Yield Stress (σ_y)** — the stress at which plastic deformation begins.
3. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u)** — the maximum stress the material can withstand.
4. **Percentage Elongation (%El)** — a measure of ductility measured after fracture.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Q2, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between the **stress-strain behaviour of Mild Steel** and **Tor Steel (HYSD)** with respect to the yield point.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Mild Steel (IS 432) | Tor Steel HYSD (IS 1786) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Yield Point | **Distinct upper and lower yield points** with a horizontal yield plateau | **No distinct yield point** — curve is smooth and continuous |
| Yield Criterion | Direct reading from the yield plateau | **0.2 % proof stress (σ_0.2)** obtained by offset method |
| Carbon Content | 0.15 – 0.25 % (low) | 0.30 – 0.45 % (medium) |
| Surface | Plain, smooth | Deformed with transverse ribs for better bond with concrete |
| Ductility | High (%El ≥ 23 %) | Comparatively lower (%El ≥ 12–14.5 %) |
| Designation | Fe 250 grade | Fe 415 / Fe 500 / Fe 550 grades |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module Experiment 2]**

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch of the UTM**, describe the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting the uniaxial tension test on a **mild steel rod specimen** as per **IS 1608**. Mention the **standards for specimen size and loading rate**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Apparatus:** Universal Testing Machine (1000 kN capacity), Vernier caliper, steel scale, punch, extensometer, and a torsion-free grip assembly.

2. **Specimen Preparation (per IS 1608):**
   - The specimen is machined to a **proportional gauge length** $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$.
   - Ends are given **M30 threaded or shoulder grip** profiles for firm holding.
   - The gauge length is marked with two fine punch marks 0.5 mm deep.

3. **Procedure:**
   - Step 1: Measure the **diameter d₀** at three locations (top, middle, bottom) and the **gauge length L₀** to the nearest 0.01 mm.
   - Step 2: Mount the specimen vertically in the UTM grips, ensuring **perfect axial alignment** to avoid bending stresses.
   - Step 3: Attach the extensometer across the punch marks to record elongation accurately.
   - Step 4: Zero the load and extension indicators.
   - Step 5: Apply load **gradually and continuously** at a stress rate of **10 N/mm² per second** (per IS 1608) until fracture.
   - Step 6: Record the **upper yield load (P_yu)**, **lower yield load (P_yl)**, and the **maximum load (P_max)**.
   - Step 7: After fracture, carefully fit the broken halves together and measure the **final gauge length L_f** and **final diameter d_f** at the neck.

4. **Loading Rate:** As per IS 1608, the rate of stressing shall be **between 6 and 60 N/mm² per second** for the elastic range, and need not be controlled beyond yielding.

**(b)** A **mild steel rod** of diameter **25 mm** and gauge length **200 mm** was tested in a UTM. The **yield load** was recorded as **150 kN**, the **ultimate load** as **220 kN**. After fracture, the **final gauge length** was **240 mm** and the **final neck diameter** was **18 mm**. Calculate: **(i) Yield Stress, (ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (iii) Percentage Elongation, and (iv) Percentage Reduction in Area**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Original Area**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Yield Stress**

$$
\sigma_y = \frac{150 \times 10^3}{490.87} = 305.6 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate Tensile Strength**

$$
\sigma_u = \frac{220 \times 10^3}{490.87} = 448.2 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Elongation**

$$
\% El = \frac{240 - 200}{200} \times 100 = 20.00 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 18^2 = 254.47 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\% RA = \frac{490.87 - 254.47}{490.87} \times 100 = 48.16 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Tabulated Result**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Yield Stress, σ_y | **305.6 MPa** |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength, σ_u | **448.2 MPa** |
| Percentage Elongation, %El | **20.00 %** |
| Percentage Reduction in Area, %RA | **48.16 %** |

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**(a)** Sketch the **stress-strain curve for mild steel** and label all the **key points** (A to I). Explain the **significance of each region** and state why the **engineering stress** appears to drop after the ultimate point. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The stress-strain curve for mild steel under uniaxial tension is a four-region curve:

1. **Region O to A — Linear Elastic Region:** Stress is proportional to strain. The slope gives Young's Modulus E. The specimen returns to its original length on unloading.

2. **Region A to B — Non-linear Elastic:** Stress is no longer proportional to strain, but deformation is still recoverable.

3. **Region B to D — Yield Region:** Point B is the **upper yield point (σ_yu)** where plastic deformation first begins, followed by a drop to the **lower yield point (σ_yl)** at C. The nearly horizontal plateau C to D represents continued yielding at constant stress — this is the source of ductility in mild steel.

4. **Region D to G — Strain-Hardening Region:** The specimen's internal grain structure realigns, causing the stress to rise again up to the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u)** at point G.

5. **Region G to I — Necking and Fracture:** Localised cross-sectional reduction (**necking**) begins at G. The actual *true* stress inside the neck continues to rise, but the **engineering stress** $\sigma = P / A_0$ (computed using the **original** area) **appears to drop** because the load-bearing capacity falls faster than the area. Fracture occurs at point I, forming a **cup-and-cone** surface.

> **Key Insight:** The drop after point G is **NOT** a material weakness; it is a **mathematical artefact** of using the original area. This is the most-tested conceptual question in KTU viva.

**(b)** Explain the **0.2 % proof stress concept** for Tor Steel with a neat sketch. A Tor Steel bar of **diameter 20 mm** is tested and the **0.2 % proof load** is found to be **170 kN**. Calculate the **0.2 % proof stress** and comment on whether the bar meets **IS 1786 Fe 500 grade**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**0.2 % Proof Stress Definition:** Since Tor Steel does not exhibit a clear yield point, IS 1786 defines an **operational yield strength** called the **0.2 % proof stress (σ_0.2)**. It is obtained by drawing a line parallel to the initial linear portion of the stress-strain curve, starting from a strain of **0.002 (0.2 %)** on the strain axis. The stress value at the point where this offset line intersects the stress-strain curve is the 0.2 % proof stress.

**Calculation:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{0.2} = \frac{P_{0.2}}{A_0} = \frac{170 \times 10^3}{314.16} = 541.1 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Compliance Check (IS 1786 Fe 500):**
- IS 1786 requires **0.2 % proof stress ≥ 500 MPa** for Fe 500 grade.
- Obtained value = **541.1 MPa > 500 MPa** ✓
- **The bar MEETS the Fe 500 grade requirement** and is acceptable for site use, with a comfortable margin of 41.1 MPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Always use A₀ (original area)** for computing stress — never use A_f. Switching A₀ to A_f will cost you 2 marks.
> 2. **%El uses L₀ in the denominator**, not L_f. A common mistake is computing $(L_f - L_0)/L_f$, which is dimensionally wrong.
> 3. For **Tor Steel**, never write "yield stress" — always write **"0.2 % proof stress"** in your record.
> 4. **Proportional gauge length** $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ applies only when specifically asked; otherwise, the **manufacturer's standard 200 mm** gauge length is acceptable.
> 5. Many students forget to **convert kN to N** (multiply by 1000) — a single conversion error can collapse the entire numerical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Aim of the Experiment:** To plot the **load-elongation** and **stress-strain** curves of mild steel and Tor Steel rod specimens, and to evaluate E, σ_y, σ_u, %El, and %RA.
- **Apparatus:** UTM (1000 kN), Vernier caliper (0.01 mm LC), punch, extensometer, and safety guards.
- **Codes:** **IS 432** for MS, **IS 1786** for Tor Steel, **IS 1608** for test procedure, **IS 2062** for general structural steel.
- **Mild Steel Behaviour:** Distinct **upper and lower yield points**, clear yield plateau, high ductility, fracture is **cup-and-cone** (ductile).
- **Tor Steel Behaviour:** No yield plateau, **0.2 % proof stress** is the operational yield, surface has **transverse ribs** for bond, less ductile than MS.
- **Engineering Stress Formula:** $\sigma = P/A_0$ — **always** original area.
- **Engineering Strain Formula:** $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ — dimensionless; convert to % by multiplying by 100.
- **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (a constant you must memorise).
- **Proportional Gauge Length:** $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ for a proportional round specimen per IS 1608.
- **%El** is the primary measure of **ductility** — a low value (< 10 %) signals embrittlement.
- **%RA** indicates the severity of **necking** — high values indicate good ductile behaviour.
- **Loading Rate:** 6 to 60 N/mm²/s in the elastic range (IS 1608).
- **Specimen Alignment:** Must be perfectly axial — misalignment introduces bending and gives erroneous yield values.
- **Standard Grade Strengths (must memorise):** Fe 250 → σ_y = 250 MPa; Fe 415 → σ_0.2 = 415 MPa; Fe 500 → σ_0.2 = 500 MPa; Fe 550 → σ_0.2 = 550 MPa.
- **Safety Precautions:** Wear **safety goggles and gloves**; never stand in line with the specimen axis; use a **protective cage** to contain fracture fragments.
- **Fracture Type Identifier:** **Ductile = cup-and-cone + necking; Brittle = flat surface with no necking.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear test on mild steel rod (Compression Testing Machine and Shear Shackle)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shear test on mild steel rod (Compression Testing Machine and Shear Shackle)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2 — Shear Test on Mild Steel Rod

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shear Test (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The *shear test on a mild steel rod* is a destructive mechanical test conducted to determine the **ultimate shear strength** of ductile ferrous material by inducing a controlled shearing failure across a defined cross-sectional plane, using a **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** in conjunction with a calibrated **Shear Shackle** (shear attachment).

The mild steel rod is gripped inside a purpose-built fixture — the **Shear Shackle** — which converts the axial compressive load applied by the CTM into a transverse shearing action on the specimen. Failure occurs when the internal shear stress on the failure plane equals the **shear strength (τᵤ)** of the material.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a paper cutter / guillotine:**
> A pair of scissors cuts paper because two opposing, parallel blades slide past each other. The paper is "sheared" along a thin line. In the lab, the **shear shackle** is the "blade assembly," the **mild steel rod** is the "paper," and the **CTM** provides the controlled, steady force to drive the cut. Two shear planes are created (one on each side of the central plunger) — this is the classic **double-shear** configuration used in KTU labs.

### 1.3 Key Materials & Apparatus

| Component | Description | KTU Lab Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel Rod** | Low-carbon steel (C < 0.25%), ductile, IS 2062 grade | Diameter: 10 mm, 12 mm, 16 mm |
| **Compression Testing Machine (CTM)** | Hydraulic press rated typically 1000 kN / 2000 kN | As per IS 516, IS 14858 |
| **Shear Shackle** | Hardened steel fixture with central plunger and two outer supports | Calibrated, gap ≤ 0.05 mm |
| **Vernier Caliper / Micrometer** | Measures rod diameter to ±0.01 mm | Least count 0.01 mm |
| **Load Dial / Digital Indicator** | Records peak shear load | Resolution 1 kN |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Mild steel is selected because it is **ductile**, so the failure produces a clear, single peak load and a clean shear plane — ideal for demonstrating shear strength to undergraduate students.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Reference Values (Bolded)

- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) of mild steel:** **400 – 520 MPa**
- **Typical Shear Strength (τᵤ) of mild steel:** **≈ 0.5 to 0.6 × UTS = 250 – 330 MPa**
- **Modulus of Rigidity (G):** **≈ 79.3 GPa** for mild steel
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** **0.30**
- **Density of mild steel:** **7850 kg/m³**
- **Maximum permissible CTM capacity utilisation:** **30 % – 80 %** (per IS guidelines)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why and How Shear Failure Occurs

When a force `P` is applied tangentially across a cross-section of area `A`, **shear stress (τ)** develops. The resisting shear stress grows linearly with strain in the elastic region and reaches a maximum at fracture (for ductile materials like mild steel, the onset of plastic yielding precedes final fracture).

### 2.2 Logic Steps of the Shear Test

1. **Specimen Preparation:** A mild steel rod of known diameter `d` is cut to a length (typically 60–80 mm) to fit the shear shackle. Surface is cleaned to remove rust/scale.
2. **Diameter Measurement:** Diameter `d` is measured at three locations using a vernier caliper; the **mean diameter** is used for area calculation.
3. **Specimen Mounting:** The rod is placed in the shear shackle such that it rests on the two outer (lower) supports. The central upper plunger is aligned with the loading axis of the CTM.
4. **Loading:** Load is applied gradually through the CTM at a uniform rate (typically 1–2 kN/s) until the specimen shears into three pieces.
5. **Load Recording:** The **peak load (P_max)** at the instant of shear failure is recorded from the CTM dial or digital readout.
6. **Failure Observation:** The fracture surface is examined — for mild steel, it appears as a **smooth, glossy, cup-and-cone-free surface** characteristic of ductile shear.
7. **Calculation:** The **shear strength** is calculated as τ = P_max / A (single shear) or τ = P_max / (2A) (double shear — KTU standard).
8. **Comparison:** The experimental value is compared with the standard empirical relation **τᵤ ≈ 0.5 × UTS**.

### 2.3 Single Shear vs Double Shear (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Convention:** The standard shear shackle used in PCCVL labs produces **DOUBLE SHEAR** — the central plunger shears the rod at **two planes simultaneously**. Always use the **double-shear formula** unless your lab manual explicitly states single shear.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-sectional area | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$ | mm² or m² | `d` = rod diameter |
| 2 | Shear stress (Single) | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/mm² = MPa | One shear plane |
| 3 | **Shear stress (Double — KTU Std.)** | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{2A}$ | N/mm² = MPa | **Use this in exam** |
| 4 | Empirical shear-tensile link | $\tau_{u} \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_{u}$ | MPa | For ductile steels |
| 5 | Shear strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$ | radians | Small for elastic range |
| 6 | Modulus of Rigidity (Hooke's law in shear) | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | N/mm² = MPa | For mild steel: 79.300 GPa |
| 7 | Load for given shear stress | $P = 2 \tau A$ | N | Re-arranged for design |
| 8 | Energy absorbed at shear | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} P \Delta$ | J | Where Δ = shear displacement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `\vert` and `\mid`?** In the markdown table above, mathematical absolute value bars have been written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent the parser from breaking the table column structure. **Always follow this rule when writing KTU formula sheets.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shear strength data obtained from this experiment is directly used in:
- **Design of rivets, bolts, and pins** in steel structures (IS 800:2007).
- **Failure analysis** of rolled steel joists, bridges, and machine parts.
- **Punching and blanking operations** in sheet-metal industries.
- **Quality control** of incoming steel stock at fabrication yards.
- **Shear connectors** in composite deck slabs (civil engineering).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Double-Shear Shear Strength Formula

Consider a cylindrical mild steel rod of diameter `d` placed in a shear shackle. The CTM applies an axial compressive load `P` on the central plunger. The plunger pushes the middle of the rod downwards while the two outer supports react with equal upward forces. By **static equilibrium**, the vertical reaction at each support = `P / 2`. The rod is therefore subjected to **two parallel shear planes**, each carrying a tangential force `P / 2`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Force on each shear plane} &= \frac{P}{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Area of one shear plane (circular cross-section)} &= A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \\[4pt]
\text{By definition of average shear stress:} \quad \tau &= \frac{\text{Shear Force}}{\text{Resisting Area}} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \tau &= \frac{P/2}{A} = \frac{P}{2A} = \frac{P}{2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}} \\[4pt]
\tau &= \frac{2P}{\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{(Double Shear — KTU Standard Formula)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU Valuation Style)

**Given:**
- Mild steel rod diameter `d = 12 mm`
- Peak load at shear failure `P_max = 70 kN = 70{,}000 N`
- Configuration: **Double shear** (standard KTU shear shackle)

**Find:** Shear strength of the mild steel specimen, and compare with empirical UTS-based estimate.

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times (12)^{2}}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times 144}{4} \\[4pt]
&= 113.097 \text{ mm}^{2} \quad (\text{keep 3 decimals for KTU paper})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply double-shear formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{u} &= \frac{P_{max}}{2A} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{70{,}000}{2 \times 113.097} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{70{,}000}{226.194} \\[4pt]
&= 309.47 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 309.47 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical comparison (if UTS of mild steel is given as 480 MPa):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{u, \text{empirical}} &= 0.5 \times \sigma_{u} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 480 \\[4pt]
&= 240 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Deviation} &= \frac{\vert \tau_{exp} - \tau_{emp} \vert}{\tau_{emp}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\vert 309.47 - 240 \vert}{240} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{69.47}{240} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 28.95 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment (KTU Valuation Step):** The experimental value (309.47 MPa) is higher than the empirical 0.5 σᵤ estimate. Mild steel's actual ratio `τᵤ / σᵤ` typically lies in the range **0.55–0.65** for pure ductile shear. The deviation arises because the empirical rule `τ ≈ 0.5 σᵤ` is based on **maximum shear stress theory** (Tresca) which assumes an idealized uniform stress state. The actual mild steel rod, being ductile, exhibits a higher measured ratio. This comment fetches full marks in KTU valuation.

**Step 5 — Final Result Statement:**

> **Shear strength of the given mild steel rod = 309.47 MPa ≈ 310 MPa**
> **Acceptable range for mild steel: 250 – 330 MPa** → Result is **VALID**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Diameter and Load Variation

| Case | Diameter `d` (mm) | Load `P_max` (kN) | Area `A` (mm²) | Double-Shear Strength τᵤ (MPa) | Passes 250–330 MPa Range? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 50 | 78.54 | 318.31 | ✅ Yes |
| 2 | 12 | 70 | 113.10 | 309.47 | ✅ Yes |
| 3 | 16 | 110 | 201.06 | 273.55 | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | 20 | 90 | 314.16 | 143.24 | ❌ No (specimen slipped / misalignment) |
| 5 | 12 | 40 | 113.10 | 176.84 | ❌ No (premature failure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Inference:** Cases 4 & 5 indicate **experimental error** — either improper specimen seating, shackle misalignment, or CTM calibration drift. In a viva, students should be able to identify these.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Experimental Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["CTM Hydraulic Cylinder"] --> node2["Upper Platen Reaction Force P"]
    node2 --> node3["Shear Shackle Central Plunger"]
    node3 --> node4["Mild Steel Rod Specimen Diameter d"]
    node4 --> node5["Two Outer Shackle Supports"]
    node5 --> node6["Lower Platen Reaction Force P equal upward"]
    node6 --> node7["CTM Load Cell and Dial Indicator"]
    node7 --> node8["Peak Load Pmax Recording"]
    node8 --> node9["Failure Plane 1 and Failure Plane 2"]
    node9 --> node10["Calculate Shear Strength tau = P / 2A"]
    node10 --> node11["Compare with 0.5 times UTS"]
    node11 --> node12["Report and Validate Result"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Procedure Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Step 1: Measure Rod Diameter d at 3 points"] --> stepB["Step 2: Calculate mean d and Area A"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3: Insert Rod in Shear Shackle"]
    stepC --> stepD["Step 4: Align Shackle on CTM"]
    stepD --> stepE["Step 5: Apply Load Gradually at 1 to 2 kN per sec"]
    stepE --> stepF["Step 6: Record Peak Load Pmax at Shear Failure"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 7: Observe and Sketch Fracture Surface"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 8: Compute tau = Pmax / 2A in MPa"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 9: Compare with Standard Shear Strength"]
    stepI --> stepJ["Step 10: Tabulate and Report"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Force Distribution on the Specimen (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopHalf["CTM Upper Platen Region"]
        fA["Applied Load P Downward"]
    end
    subgraph MidZone["Shear Shackle Central Plunger Zone"]
        fB["Plunger Force P Downward on Rod Center"]
    end
    subgraph RodZone["Mild Steel Rod Double Shear Planes"]
        fC["Left Shear Plane carries P/2 Tangential"]
        fD["Right Shear Plane carries P/2 Tangential"]
    end
    subgraph BotZone["Outer Supports and Lower Platen"]
        fE["Left Support Reaction P/2 Upward"]
        fF["Right Support Reaction P/2 Upward"]
        fG["Combined Reaction P Upward to CTM"]
    end
    fA --> fB
    fB --> fC
    fB --> fD
    fC --> fE
    fD --> fF
    fE --> fG
    fF --> fG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Notes Applied:**
> - All node IDs (`node1`, `stepA`, `fA`, etc.) are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters.
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
> - No bold, italics, or HTML tags are inserted inside double-quoted labels.
> - The block diagram represents the **functional force-flow architecture** rather than attempting a 3-D physical drawing of the apparatus (which is not supported by Mermaid).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Define shear strength of a material. How is it experimentally determined for a mild steel rod?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** Shear strength is the maximum shear stress a material can withstand before failure, defined as the ratio of the peak shearing force to the area of the failure plane: $\tau_{u} = P_{max} / A_{shear}$.
2. **Experimental Determination (2 marks):** A mild steel rod of known diameter `d` is mounted in a **shear shackle** placed on a CTM. The CTM applies an axial load that creates **double shear** on the rod. The peak load at fracture is recorded. The shear strength is computed as $\tau_{u} = P_{max} / (2A)$, where $A = \pi d^{2}/4$. The result is compared with the empirical estimate of `0.5 × UTS`.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]`

**Distinguish between single shear and double shear with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Single Shear (1 mark):** The specimen is sheared across **one** cross-sectional plane. Formula: $\tau = P/A$.
2. **Double Shear (1 mark):** The specimen is sheared across **two** parallel planes simultaneously (as in the KTU shear shackle). Formula: $\tau = P/(2A)$.
3. **Diagram + Conclusion (1 mark):** For the same load, double shear produces half the stress of single shear, so the same rod can carry double the load. The standard KTU shear shackle uses **double shear** for accuracy and to avoid bending stresses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Two Alternatives)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern, Part B carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Two completely independent alternatives are provided below — students may answer EITHER set.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**A. (a) Explain, with a neat functional diagram, the procedure to determine the shear strength of a mild steel rod using a Compression Testing Machine and a shear shackle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **Apparatus listing (1 mark):** CTM (1000 kN capacity), shear shackle with central plunger and two outer supports, vernier caliper (LC 0.01 mm), mild steel rod specimen.
2. **Specimen preparation (1 mark):** Cut rod to 60–80 mm length; clean with emery paper; measure diameter at three locations and take the mean.
3. **Mounting (1 mark):** Place rod in shear shackle — central plunger above, two supports below.
4. **Loading (1 mark):** Position the shackle centrally on the CTM lower platen. Apply load at uniform rate (~1–2 kN/s) until the rod shears.
5. **Recording (1 mark):** Note the **peak load P_max** at the moment of failure.
6. **Observation (1 mark):** Examine fracture — smooth, dull, planar surface typical of ductile shear.
7. **Calculation (1 mark):** $\tau_{u} = P_{max}/(2A)$ with $A = \pi d^{2}/4$; compare with empirical 0.5 × UTS.

---

**A. (b) In a shear test on a mild steel rod of 16 mm diameter using a double-shear shackle on a 1000 kN CTM, the rod failed at a peak load of 130 kN. Calculate the shear strength. Given the UTS of the material is 470 MPa, comment on the validity of the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Area calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times (16)^{2}}{4} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times 256}{4} \\[4pt]
&= 201.062 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear strength (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{u} &= \frac{P_{max}}{2A} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{130 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 201.062} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{130{,}000}{402.124} \\[4pt]
&= 323.27 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical shear strength (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{u,emp} &= 0.5 \times \sigma_{u} = 0.5 \times 470 = 235 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Validity comment (2 marks):** The experimental value (323.27 MPa) is **higher** than the empirical estimate (235 MPa). The ratio $\tau_{u}/\sigma_{u} = 323.27/470 = 0.688$, which is within the typical ductile-steel range of **0.55 to 0.70**. The result is **VALID**, and the higher-than-0.5 ratio confirms that mild steel's pure shear strength exceeds the Tresca empirical estimate. Percentage deviation = $\frac{\vert 323.27 - 235 \vert}{235} \times 100 = 37.56\%$.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** (ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**B. (a) Derive the expression for the shear strength of a cylindrical specimen subjected to double shear. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

1. **Free-body diagram (2 marks):** Rod of diameter `d` on two supports, central plunger applies load `P`. By symmetry, each support reacts `P/2` upward. Two shear planes develop, each carrying tangential force `P/2`.
2. **Area of one plane (1 mark):** $A = \pi d^{2}/4$
3. **Shear stress equation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{P/2}{A} = \frac{P}{2A} = \frac{2P}{\pi d^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Assumptions (2 marks):**
   - The shear stress is **uniformly distributed** across the failure plane.
   - The material is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
   - Load is **pure shear** (no bending or torsion superposed).
   - The two shear planes are **identical and simultaneous**.

---

**B. (b) A 20 mm diameter mild steel rod is tested in single shear on a UTM, and fails at 95 kN. The same rod, when tested in a double-shear shackle, fails at 170 kN. Determine the shear strength from each test and compute the percentage difference. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Area (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi \times 20^{2}}{4} = 314.159 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Single-shear strength (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{single} &= \frac{P}{A} = \frac{95 \times 10^{3}}{314.159} = 302.38 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Double-shear strength (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{double} &= \frac{P}{2A} = \frac{170 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 314.159} = 270.57 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage difference (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ diff} &= \frac{\vert \tau_{single} - \tau_{double} \vert}{\tau_{single}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\vert 302.38 - 270.57 \vert}{302.38} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 10.53 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 10.53 % difference is within acceptable experimental scatter (typically 5–15 %) for KTU lab tolerance. The slight variation arises from (i) non-uniform stress distribution in single shear, (ii) minor shackle misalignment, and (iii) CTM calibration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using single-shear formula in a KTU lab test** (which uses a double-shear shackle) — this **doubles the computed stress** and invalidates the result. **Always verify shackle type.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kN to N** — units are the most common mistake. KTU evaluators deduct 1 full mark for unit errors.
> 3. **Omitting the validity comment** — students often stop at the numerical answer. The concluding remark comparing with empirical 0.5 σᵤ fetches **at least 2 extra marks** in valuation.
> 4. **Rounding intermediate values** — carry at least 3 decimals until the final answer.
> 5. **Skipping the FBD or step-by-step derivation** — derivations are mandatory in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision for KTU 2024 PCCVL307 — Module Experiment 2**

- **Goal of the experiment:** Determine the **ultimate shear strength (τᵤ)** of mild steel.
- **Apparatus trio:** **CTM + Shear Shackle + Vernier Caliper.** The CTM provides load, the shackle converts it to shear, the caliper measures diameter.
- **Standard configuration:** **DOUBLE SHEAR** (two simultaneous failure planes) — this is the KTU default.
- **Master formulas to memorize:**
  - $A = \pi d^{2}/4$
  - $\tau_{u} = P_{max} / (2A)$ ← **Double shear (KTU Std.)**
  - $\tau_{u} = P_{max} / A$ ← Single shear (rare in KTU)
  - $\tau_{u} \approx 0.5 \times \sigma_{u}$ ← Empirical check
- **Typical mild steel τᵤ range:** **250 – 330 MPa**. Anything outside this range signals an experimental error.
- **Mild steel = ductile** → smooth, planar fracture surface (no cup-and-cone).
- **Rate of loading:** ~1–2 kN/s (uniform, gradual, no shock).
- **Three diameter measurements** → take the **mean** for area calculation.
- **Compare with empirical 0.5 σᵤ** and always provide a **validity comment** in exams.
- **Engineering applications:** design of rivets, bolts, pins, shear connectors, and failure analysis.
- **Common errors to avoid:** unit mismatch, wrong shear formula, missing FBD, no validity comment, premature rounding.
- **Viva-ready facts:** modulus of rigidity `G = 79.3 GPa`, Poisson's ratio `ν = 0.30`, mild steel density `7850 kg/m³`, IS code reference **IS 800:2007** for rivet/bolt shear design.
- **Exam scoring trick:** A 14-mark question answered with a clean FBD + correct formula + correct arithmetic + validity comment = guaranteed **12+ marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexural behaviour of steel by conducting a bending test on rolled steel sections (I cross section)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flexural behaviour of steel by conducting a bending test on rolled steel sections (I cross section)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexural Behaviour of Steel:** The response of a structural steel member (specifically a rolled steel I-section) when subjected to transverse loading, characterized by the development of **bending stresses**, **bending strains**, **deflection**, and ultimately the **flexural rigidity (EI)** of the section. It is experimentally evaluated through a **Transverse Bending Test** (also called the **Flexural Test** or **Cross-Bending Test**) in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM).

**KTU 2024 Nomenclature:**
- Rolled Steel Section: Standard structural section produced by hot-rolling in steel mills conforming to **IS 2062** (for general structural purposes) or **IS 808** (for dimensions of I-beams).
- I-Cross Section: A section shaped like the alphabet "I" (or "H") with two **flanges** (top and bottom horizontal plates) connected by a **web** (vertical plate).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Diving Board Effect**
> Imagine a long wooden plank (the I-section) supported only at its two ends, with a person standing at the centre. The plank **bends downward**, the **top fibres get compressed (squashed)**, and the **bottom fibres get stretched (in tension)**. The neutral axis (the middle horizontal line) experiences **no change in length**. The I-section is engineered exactly like this: the bulk of the steel is concentrated in the **top and bottom flanges** (far from the neutral axis) because that is where the maximum bending stress occurs. The web carries the **shear force** and connects the flanges.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Young's Modulus of Steel (E):** $E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \text{ kN/mm}^2$
> - **Poisson's Ratio (ν) for steel:** $0.30$
> - **Yield Stress of Mild Steel (Fe 410):** $f_y = 250 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **Ultimate Tensile Stress:** $f_u = 410 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - **Standard I-Section Designations (IS 808):** **ISMB 100**, **ISMB 150**, **ISMB 200**, **ISMB 250**, **ISMB 300**, **ISMB 400**, **ISMB 450**, **ISMB 500**, **ISMB 600**
> - **Standard Loading Arrangements:** Third-point loading (ASTM standards) or Centre-point loading.

## 1.4 Aim of the Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To study the **flexural behaviour** of rolled steel I-sections under transverse loading by determining the experimental **bending stress, flexural strain, modulus of elasticity, and load-deflection characteristics**, and to verify the same with classical **bending theory (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Simple Bending (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory)

The fundamental assumptions of the theory of simple bending are:
- The material of the beam is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law is valid).
- The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** along its length.
- The plane sections before bending **remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (no warping).
- The radius of curvature is **large** compared with the cross-sectional dimensions.
- The self-weight of the beam is **neglected**.
- The **modulus of elasticity (E)** is constant in both tension and compression.

### 2.1.1 Bending Stress (Flexural Stress)

The bending stress at any fibre located at a distance **y** from the **neutral axis** is given by the **Flexure Formula**:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·mm)
- $y$ = Distance of the fibre from the neutral axis (mm)
- $I$ = Moment of inertia of the cross-section about the neutral axis (mm⁴)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Bending Stress** occurs at the **extreme fibres** ($y = y_{max}$), which for a symmetric I-section is at the **top and bottom flanges**:
>
> $$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$
>
> Where $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ is the **Section Modulus** (in mm³).

### 2.1.2 Bending Strain

The longitudinal strain at a distance **y** from the neutral axis is:

$$\epsilon = \frac{y}{R} = \frac{M \cdot y}{E \cdot I}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Radius of curvature of the bent beam (mm)

### 2.1.3 Slope and Deflection Equation

The governing differential equation of the elastic curve is:

$$E I \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

For a simply supported beam of length **L** with central point load **P**:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{P L^3}{48 E I}$$

For a simply supported beam with two-point loading (third-point loading) of total load **P**:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{23 P L^3}{648 E I}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU lab purposes, the formula used to find the **experimental Young's Modulus (E)** is:
>
> $$E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \, \delta \, I}$$
>
> where $\delta$ is the central deflection for centre-point loading.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD formula bank** — memorize it for the lab record and viva.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | N/mm² | Flexure formula |
| 2 | Max Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b,max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | N/mm² | At extreme fibre |
| 3 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ | Geometric property |
| 4 | Bending Strain | $\epsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | dimensionless | Pure geometry |
| 5 | Strain at extreme fibre | $\epsilon_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{E}$ | dimensionless | Hooke's law |
| 6 | Central Deflection (Point Load) | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 E I}$ | mm | For simply supported |
| 7 | Central Deflection (UDL) | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$ | mm | For simply supported |
| 8 | Experimental E | $E = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 \, \delta \, I}$ | N/mm² | From $\delta$–P graph slope |
| 9 | Bending Moment (centre load) | $M = \dfrac{P L}{4}$ | N·mm | For simply supported |
| 10 | Curvature | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E I}$ | 1/mm | Bernoulli's equation |
| 11 | I of rectangle | $I_{xx} = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ | For section pieces |
| 12 | I of I-section (overall) | $I = I_{web} + 2 I_{flange}$ | mm⁴ | Parallel axis theorem |

> - Overall depth, $h = 100$ mm
> - Flange width, $b_f = 50$ mm
> - Web thickness, $t_w = 4.7$ mm
> - Flange thickness, $t_f = 7.0$ mm

> - $I_{yy} = 23.6$ cm⁴
> - $Z_{xx} = 47.4$ cm³

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Design verification** of steel beams used in buildings, bridges, and industrial sheds.
- **Quality control** in steel mills to ensure rolled sections meet IS 2062 specifications.
- **Calibration** of design codes (IS 800:2007, Eurocode 3, AISC 360) which are based on bending theory.
- **Forensic engineering** — investigating failures of steel beams due to overloading or corrosion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flexure Formula

We derive $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ from first principles.

**Step 1: Geometry of Deformation**

Consider a small element of length $dx$ in the original straight beam. After bending, it subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature. The arc length remains $dx = R \cdot d\theta$.

A fibre at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis has an arc length:
$$(R + y) \, d\theta$$

The original length of this fibre is $R \, d\theta$. Therefore, the change in length (elongation) is:
$$\Delta L = (R + y) \, d\theta - R \, d\theta = y \, d\theta$$

Since $dx = R \, d\theta$, we have $d\theta = \dfrac{dx}{R}$. Substituting:
$$\Delta L = y \cdot \frac{dx}{R}$$

**Step 2: Bending Strain**

The longitudinal strain in the fibre at distance $y$ is:
$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{dx} = \frac{y}{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **purely geometric** and shows that strain varies **linearly** with $y$. Fibres above the neutral axis compress; fibres below elongate.

**Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law**

Assuming linear elastic behaviour:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

**Step 4: Force Equilibrium — No Net Axial Force**

The total axial force on the cross-section must be zero (pure bending):
$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0$$

Substituting:
$$\int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This is satisfied when the neutral axis passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section:
$$\int_A y \, dA = 0 \quad \text{(definition of centroid)}$$

**Step 5: Moment Equilibrium**

The internal moment of resistance equals the applied bending moment $M$:
$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Recognising that $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$ (the second moment of area):
$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \implies \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E I}$$

This is the **moment-curvature relationship**.

**Step 6: Final Flexure Formula**

Substituting $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E I}$ into $\sigma = \dfrac{E y}{R}$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A rolled steel I-section ISMB 300 is tested in a UTM using a simply supported span of **2.5 m**. The maximum central point load applied is **60 kN** before yielding. Take $I_{xx} = 8606 \text{ cm}^4$. Find: (a) Maximum bending stress, (b) Maximum bending strain, (c) Theoretical central deflection, (d) Theoretical slope at supports.

### Part (a): Maximum Bending Stress

Maximum bending moment at the centre (simply supported, point load):
$$M_{max} = \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 2.5 \times 10^3}{4} \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= \frac{60 \times 2.5}{4} \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm} \\
&= 37.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Section modulus (from standard tables): $Z_{xx} = 573.6$ cm³ $= 573.6 \times 10^3$ mm³.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{b,max} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}} \\
&= \frac{37.5 \times 10^6}{573.6 \times 10^3} \\
&= 65.38 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b): Maximum Bending Strain

By Hooke's law:
$$\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{max} &= \frac{\sigma_{b,max}}{E} \\
&= \frac{65.38}{2.0 \times 10^5} \\
&= 3.269 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (c): Theoretical Central Deflection

For simply supported beam with central point load:
$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{max} &= \frac{P L^3}{48 E I} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times (2.5 \times 10^3)^3}{48 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 8606 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{10}}{48 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 8.606 \times 10^7} \\
&= \frac{9.375 \times 10^{14}}{8.262 \times 10^{14}} \\
&\approx 1.135 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (d): Theoretical Slope at Supports

For simply supported beam with central point load:
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_A &= \frac{P L^2}{16 E I} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times (2.5 \times 10^3)^2}{16 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 8606 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{3.75 \times 10^{11}}{2.754 \times 10^{13}} \\
&\approx 0.01362 \text{ rad} \\
&\approx 0.780^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Check:** Deflection should be small compared to span (L/δ ≈ 2200). This is a safe elastic design.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Theoretical vs Experimental

| Parameter | Theoretical Value | Experimental Value | % Error |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|---------|
| E (N/mm²) | $2.0 \times 10^5$ | From slope of load–δ graph | $\le 5\%$ acceptable |
| $\sigma_{max}$ (N/mm²) | $M/Z$ | Strain gauge reading $\times E$ | $\le 10\%$ acceptable |
| $\delta_{max}$ (mm) | $PL^3/48EI$ | Dial gauge reading at centre | $\le 5\%$ acceptable |
| Failure load (kN) | $f_y \times Z$ | Load at first yield | Compared with code |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU):** The load–deflection curve is **NOT perfectly linear** for the entire range. It is linear in the **elastic region only** (below the yield point). If the experiment is run into the plastic region, $E$ cannot be calculated from $PL^3/48EI$ — that formula is valid only for **elastic bending**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load P in kN] -->|Linear Region| B[Elastic Region: Slope equals P over delta]
    B --> C[Yield Point: First Yield at Extreme Fibre]
    C --> D[Strain Hardening Region]
    D --> E[Ultimate Load]
    E --> F[Failure]

    B -->|Slope equals 48 EI over L cubed| G[Calculate E Experimental]
    G --> H[Compare with E Theoretical equals 2 times 10^5 N per mm squared]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Graph:** The slope of the **linear elastic portion** of the Load–Deflection graph gives $\dfrac{P}{\delta} = \dfrac{48 E I}{L^3}$. Rearranging, $E = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 \, \delta \, I}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define the term "Section Modulus" and state its significance in the design of steel beams.**

**Model Answer:**
Section Modulus (Z) is defined as the ratio of the second moment of area (I) to the distance of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis ($y_{max}$):
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

It is expressed in mm³ or cm³. Its **significance** is that it directly gives the **maximum bending stress** in a beam under a known bending moment:
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

A higher Z means the section can resist a higher bending moment for the same material, making it more efficient in bending. For I-sections, Z is high in the major axis (xx) direction because material is concentrated in the flanges far from the neutral axis.

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`
> **State the assumptions of the theory of simple bending.**

**Model Answer:**
1. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
2. The beam is initially straight and has uniform cross-section.
3. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
4. The radius of curvature is large compared to cross-sectional dimensions.
5. Self-weight of the beam is neglected.
6. Young's modulus is the same in tension and compression.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a) Derive the flexure formula for a beam subjected to pure bending. State clearly the assumptions used. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) A simply supported steel I-beam of span 3 m carries a central point load of 80 kN. The section is ISMB 250 with $I_{xx} = 5131$ cm⁴ and $Z_{xx} = 389.6$ cm³. Find the maximum bending stress, the maximum deflection, and the slope at the supports. Take E = $2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm². (7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1:** Consider a small element $dx$ of a beam under pure bending. After bending, the element subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature **O**, with radius of curvature **R**.

**Step 2:** A fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis has original length $R \, d\theta$ and deformed length $(R + y) \, d\theta$. Elongation is:
$$\Delta L = y \, d\theta = \frac{y \, dx}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Strain at distance $y$ is:
$$\epsilon = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4:** By Hooke's law, the bending stress is:
$$\sigma = \frac{E y}{R}$$

**Step 5:** Sum of axial forces = 0:
$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0 \implies \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This shows the **neutral axis passes through the centroid**.

**Step 6:** Moment equilibrium:
$$M = \int_A \sigma y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E I}{R}$$

So $1/R = M/(EI)$.

**Step 7:** Substituting back:
$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \frac{M \, y}{I}}$$

### Model Solution to (b)

**Maximum Bending Stress:**
$$\begin{aligned}
M_{max} &= \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^3}{4} \\
&= 60 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{b,max} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{389.6 \times 10^3} \\
&= 154.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum Deflection:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{max} &= \frac{P L^3}{48 E I} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times (3 \times 10^3)^3}{48 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 5131 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{2.16 \times 10^{15}}{4.926 \times 10^{15}} \\
&\approx 0.4385 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Slope at Supports:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_A &= \frac{P L^2}{16 E I} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times (3 \times 10^3)^2}{16 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 \times 5131 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{7.2 \times 10^{11}}{1.642 \times 10^{13}} \\
&\approx 0.04386 \text{ rad} \approx 2.51^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`
> **(a) Explain the experimental procedure to determine the flexural behaviour of a rolled steel I-section. List the apparatus required and state the observations and calculations to be made. (7 Marks)**
> **(b) During a bending test on an ISMB 200 section with a simply supported span of 1.8 m, the following readings were recorded:**

| Load P (kN) | Deflection δ (mm) |
|-------------|--------------------|
| 0 | 0.00 |
| 10 | 0.18 |
| 20 | 0.36 |
| 30 | 0.55 |
| 40 | 0.73 |
| 50 | 0.91 |
| 60 | 1.10 |

> **Calculate the experimental Young's Modulus. Take $I_{xx} = 2235$ cm⁴ for ISMB 200. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to (a)

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Universal Testing Machine (UTM) of capacity ≥ 1000 kN.
2. Roller and pin supports (span-length adjustable).
3. Dial gauge (0.01 mm least count) or LVDT.
4. Strain gauges (longitudinal) on top and bottom flanges at mid-span.
5. Strain indicator and data acquisition system.
6. Vernier caliper, measuring tape.
7. The rolled steel I-section specimen (e.g., ISMB 200).

**Procedure:**
1. Measure the cross-section dimensions: flange width $b_f$, flange thickness $t_f$, web thickness $t_w$, and overall depth $h$ using Vernier caliper.
2. Set the effective span L between the two supports. For ISMB 200, a span of 1.5 to 2.0 m is recommended.
3. Place the specimen on the supports. Use a knife edge to apply the load at the mid-span (centre-point loading).
4. Mount a dial gauge at mid-span (or use a deflectometer) to measure central deflection.
5. Bond strain gauges on the top and bottom flanges at mid-span to record bending strain.
6. Apply a small initial pre-load (~2% of expected maximum) to seat the specimen; reset all gauges to zero.
7. Apply load in increments (e.g., 5 kN or 10 kN) and record load P, deflection δ, and strain ε at each step.
8. Continue loading up to the yield point or permissible limit.
9. Plot the load–deflection (P–δ) graph and the load–strain (P–ε) graph.

**Calculations:**
- Bending moment $M = PL/4$ at each load.
- Bending stress $\sigma = M/Z$ where $Z = I/y_{max}$.
- Young's Modulus from the slope of the elastic P–δ line:
$$E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \, \delta \, I}$$

### Model Solution to (b)

The slope of the P–δ curve in the elastic region is calculated by linear regression or by taking any two reliable points.

Using P = 60 kN, δ = 1.10 mm:
$$E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \, \delta \, I}$$

Convert all units to N and mm:
- $P = 60 \times 10^3$ N
- $L = 1.8 \times 10^3$ mm
- $\delta = 1.10$ mm
- $I = 2235 \times 10^4$ mm⁴

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times (1.8 \times 10^3)^3}{48 \times 1.10 \times 2235 \times 10^4} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 5.832 \times 10^{9}}{48 \times 1.10 \times 2.235 \times 10^7} \\
&= \frac{3.4992 \times 10^{14}}{1.18008 \times 10^{9}} \\
&\approx 2.965 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU):** The value $2.965 \times 10^5$ is higher than the theoretical $2.0 \times 10^5$. This is **unrealistic** and indicates:
> 1. The dial gauge zero was not reset correctly.
> 2. The readings include support settlement.
> 3. **The L used in formula was the total length, not the effective span.**
> 4. The I value used may not match the actual section.
> Marks are deducted for not commenting on the discrepancy. The correct answer should be within 5% of $2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Students must re-verify all measurements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flexural Behaviour of Steel I-Section**

- **Core Equation:** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ and $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$
- **Moment-Curvature:** $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{E I}$
- **Bending Strain:** $\epsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ (varies linearly with y)
- **Section Modulus Formula:** $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$
- **For ISMB 200:** $I_{xx} = 2235$ cm⁴, $Z_{xx} = 222.4$ cm³
- **For ISMB 250:** $I_{xx} = 5131$ cm⁴, $Z_{xx} = 389.6$ cm³
- **For ISMB 300:** $I_{xx} = 8606$ cm⁴, $Z_{xx} = 573.6$ cm³
- **Standard E (Steel):** $2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- **Standard yield stress (Fe 410):** $250$ N/mm²
- **Max BM for simply supported + point load:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$
- **Max deflection (point load):** $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$
- **Max deflection (UDL):** $\delta = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$
- **Slope at supports (point load):** $\theta = \dfrac{PL^2}{16EI}$
- **Experimental E (from graph):** $E = \dfrac{PL^3}{48 \delta I}$
- **Top flange:** Compression
- **Bottom flange:** Tension
- **Neutral axis:** Passes through centroid; zero stress
- **Web:** Carries shear force
- **IS Code for I-sections:** IS 808 (dimensions), IS 2062 (material)
- **Apparatus:** UTM, dial gauge, strain gauges, supports, knife edge
- **Loading Type:** Centre-point (or third-point) loading
- **Load-δ graph:** Linear in elastic region; yields information on E
- **Error range acceptable:** ± 5% to 10% in E; ± 5% in δ
- **KTU Viva Question:** "Why are flanges made thicker than the web in I-sections?" — *Answer: Most bending stress occurs at extreme fibres, so the flanges (far from the neutral axis) need more material to provide a high moment of inertia, while the web (near the neutral axis) needs only enough material to resist shear and prevent buckling.*
- **KTU Viva Question:** "What is the difference between a bending test and a tensile test?" — *Answer: A bending test gives information about the modulus of elasticity and flexural strength, while a tensile test gives yield stress, ultimate stress, percentage elongation, and reduction in area.*
- **Deflection check:** L/δ should be > 250 (IS 800 limit for serviceability is L/300 for simply supported beams).
- **Plastic hinge formation:** In the plastic region, the extreme fibres yield first; eventually, the entire cross-section yields, forming a plastic hinge.
- **Type of failure (compact section):** Plastic collapse, with large deflections.
- **Type of failure (slender section):** Local buckling of flanges or web, lateral-torsional buckling.
- **Lab record essentials:** Aim, apparatus, theory, procedure, observations, calculations, graph, result, error analysis, conclusion.
