- **Endurance ratio** for un-notched steels: $\sigma_e / \sigma_u \approx 0.5$ (empirical rule of thumb, useful for KTU design problems).
- **Three stages of fatigue failure:** crack initiation (persistent slip bands) → crack propagation (striations) → final fracture (rough crystalline region).
- **Beach marks** (macro) and **striations** (micro) are both diagnostic features of the fatigue fracture surface.
- **Surface finish is critical** — polished specimens give 30-50% higher fatigue life than rough-ground specimens at the same stress level.
- **Notch sensitivity** reduces fatigue life; use **Neuber's equation** or **Peterson's charts** for notched components.
- The standard codes are **ASTM E466 / E468** and **IS 5074 : 1994** — always cite at least one in your answer.
- The KTU lab exam typically asks: (i) a definition (3 marks), (ii) the formula derivation (4 marks), (iii) a numerical S-N problem (4 marks), and (iv) a sketch of the S-N curve with the endurance limit marked (3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study/demonstration of strain gauges and load cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study/demonstration of strain gauges and load cells # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Strain Gauge — Formal Definition A **strain gauge** (also called a *strain gage* or *strain transducer*) is a passive resistive sensor whose electrical resistance changes in a precisely proportional manner when it is mechanically elongated or compressed. The change in resistance $\Delta R$ is directly related to the mechanical strain $\varepsilon$ experienced by the surface to which the gauge is bonded, as defined by the **gauge factor** $GF$: $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A strain gauge is a sensor used to measure the strain (deformation) of an object. It works on the principle that the electrical resistance of a conductor changes when it is stretched or compressed. It is the **fundamental building block** of virtually all force, load, pressure, and torque transducers used in modern civil and mechanical engineering. ## 1.2 Load Cell — Formal Definition A **load cell** is a *force transducer* that converts an applied mechanical force (tension or compression) into a measurable, calibrated electrical output signal — most commonly a low-level DC voltage (typically **mV/V**). The most prevalent construction uses a precisely machined elastic element (the *load-bearing body*) instrumented with strain gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Output Range:** Most industrial load cells produce a **rated output of 2 mV/V to 3 mV/V**, meaning at full rated load and 10 V excitation, the output is **20 mV to 30 mV**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Band Resistance" Intuition Imagine a cheap party balloon: blow it up, and the thin rubber stretches — it gets *longer* and *thinner*. Now imagine this stretched rubber was actually a tiny electrical wire. A wire that is **longer** has *more* resistance (electrons travel a longer path), and a wire that is **thinner** has *more* resistance (less cross-sectional area for electrons to pass). So when a wire stretches, its resistance **increases predictably**. A **strain gauge is nothing but a very thin, zig-zag-patterned wire** (or metallic foil) glued onto a surface. When the surface bends, stretches, or compresses, the zig-zag wire stretches with it — and its tiny resistance change (a few milliohms!) is precisely measured using a **Wheatstone bridge**. A **load cell** is essentially a *metal block* (like a stubby dumbbell) that has strain gauges glued to it. When you push or pull on the block, it deforms microscopically (it never visibly bends), the strain gauges change resistance, and electronics convert that change into a digital force reading. Think of it as a **"smart bathroom scale"** — but engineered for laboratory or industrial precision. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Connection:** The same principle is used in: > - Truck weighbridges (road load measurement) > - Aircraft wing fatigue monitoring > - Bridge structural health monitoring > - Robotic force-feedback grippers > - Hydraulic press force verification ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Gauge Factor (GF) for metallic foil gauges:** **2.0 to 2.2** (typical = **2.0**) - **Gauge Factor for semiconductor gauges:** **±100 to ±200** - **Standard gauge resistance:** **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 1000 Ω** (most common = **350 Ω**) - **Bridge excitation voltage:** **5 V, 10 V, or 12 V DC** - **Youngs Modulus of gauge material (constantan):** ~**162 GPa** - **Poissons ratio of gauge material (constantan):** ~**0.3** - **Strain range of typical foil gauges:** **±1% to ±5%** (±10,000 to ±50,000 microstrain) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Bonded Metallic Strain Gauge The strain gauge operates on **three coupled physical effects**: 1. **Geometric Effect:** Stretching increases length $L$ and decreases cross-sectional area $A$, both increasing resistance: $$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$ 2. **Piezoresistive Effect:** The resistivity $\rho$ of the conductor material itself changes slightly with strain. The total effect is captured by the **gauge factor**: $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$ where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio of the gauge material and the last term is the piezoresistive component. 3. **Bonded Constraint:** A strong adhesive layer forces the gauge wire to deform *identically* with the host specimen — transferring strain with virtually no slip. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a "zig-zag" pattern?** The grid pattern **multiplies the effective length** $L$ of wire inside a small area, thereby *amplifying* $\Delta R$ for the same strain, while keeping the gauge compact. Each straight segment of the grid contributes strain in the **active axis** (the long direction of the grid), while the *return loops* at the ends are oriented perpendicular (sensitive to transverse strain — usually minimized or compensated). ## 2.2 The Wheatstone Bridge — Why It Is Indispensable A single strain gauge produces a *tiny* resistance change (e.g., $R = 350\ \Omega$, $\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon$, $GF = 2.0 \Rightarrow \Delta R = 0.7\ \Omega$). Measuring $\Delta R$ directly is impractical. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts this small resistance imbalance into a measurable voltage. **Bridge Configuration:** ``` R1 R2 +---/\/\---+---/\/\---+ | | | Vin Vout GND | | | +---/\/\---+---/\/\---+ R3 R4 ``` The output voltage is: $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$ In a **balanced bridge** ($R_1R_4 = R_2R_3$), $V_{out} = 0$. Any strain-induced $\Delta R$ unbalances the bridge, producing a small $V_{out}$ proportional to strain. ## 2.3 Bridge Arrangements Used in Load Cells | Configuration | Active Gauges | Output (mV/V) for strain $\varepsilon$ | Temperature Compensation | Bending Compensation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Quarter Bridge** | 1 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4(1 + GF \cdot \varepsilon)}$ | No | No | | **Half Bridge** | 2 (adjacent arms) | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{2}$ | Partial | Yes (if Poisson arms) | | **Full Bridge** | 4 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | **Full** | **Full** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Most commercial load cells use a **Poisson-bridge** arrangement (full bridge with two gauges in tension, two in compression from Poisson effect) because it gives **4× the sensitivity** of a quarter bridge *and* automatically cancels thermal expansion and bending artefacts. ## 2.4 Load Cell Components and Construction A typical **S-type / shear-web / column-type strain-gauge load cell** consists of: 1. **Load-bearing element (spring element):** A precisely machined steel or aluminium body designed to deform elastically (and *only* elastically — within the proportional limit) under load. 2. **Strain gauges:** 4 (or more) bonded strategically at points of maximum stress concentration — typically on a machined *flexure* or *shear web*. 3. **Wheatstone bridge network:** All 4 gauges interconnected and sealed within the cell. 4. **Hermetic seal / environmental protection:** Welded cover with cable gland, often IP67 / IP68 rated. 5. **Cable with shielded conductors:** For excitation, output signal, and sense lines (for 6-wire load cells). 6. **Mounting hardware:** Threaded holes, eye bolts, or button load caps. ## 2.5 Load Cell Specifications and Performance Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **Capacity (Rated Load)** | $F_{max}$ | 1 N to 1000+ tonnes | Maximum safe load | | **Rated Output (Sensitivity)** | $S$ | 2.0 mV/V | Output per volt excitation at full load | | **Non-linearity** | $NL$ | ±0.05% of FS | Deviation from straight calibration line | | **Hysteresis** | $H$ | ±0.05% of FS | Difference between loading and unloading | | **Non-repeatability** | $NR$ | ±0.05% of FS | Variation between repeated measurements | | **Creep (30 min)** | $Cr$ | ±0.05% of FS | Drift under sustained load | | **Temperature effect on zero** | $T_0$ | ±0.05% FS/°C | Zero shift with temperature | | **Temperature effect on span** | $T_S$ | ±0.05% FS/°C | Sensitivity shift with temperature | | **Safe Overload** | — | 150% of capacity | Load before permanent damage | | **Ultimate Overload** | — | 300% of capacity | Load before mechanical failure | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Experiment Matters - **Civil Engineering:** Structural load testing, pile load tests, bridge weigh-in-motion, post-tensioning jack calibration - **Mechanical Engineering:** Machine tool force monitoring, fatigue testing rigs, press calibration - **Aerospace:** Wind tunnel balance calibration, landing gear load verification - **Process Industries:** Tank weighing, hopper scales, batching systems - **Biomedical:** Prosthetic force sensors, gait analysis platforms # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Change in Resistance of a Strained Wire **Given:** A uniform wire of original length $L_0$, cross-sectional area $A_0$, and resistivity $\rho_0$. **Step 1 — Write the baseline resistance:** $$R_0 = \rho_0 \cdot \frac{L_0}{A_0}$$ **Step 2 — Apply an axial strain $\varepsilon$ along the length:** The new length becomes: $$L = L_0(1 + \varepsilon)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Poisson effect transversely:** The new cross-sectional area (assuming circular wire of diameter $d$): $$d = d_0(1 - \nu\varepsilon) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = A_0(1 - \nu\varepsilon)^2$$ **Step 4 — The resistivity changes by the piezoresistive factor:** $$\rho = \rho_0(1 + C\varepsilon)$$ where $C = \dfrac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$ is the *piezoresistive coefficient*. **Step 5 — Substitute into $R$ to get the strained resistance:** $$R = \rho_0(1 + C\varepsilon) \cdot \frac{L_0(1 + \varepsilon)}{A_0(1 - \nu\varepsilon)^2}$$ **Step 6 — Divide by the original $R_0$ to obtain the fractional change:** $$\frac{R}{R_0} = (1 + C\varepsilon) \cdot (1 + \varepsilon) \cdot (1 - \nu\varepsilon)^{-2}$$ **Step 7 — Apply the small-strain approximation** $\varepsilon \ll 1$, so $(1 - \nu\varepsilon)^{-2} \approx 1 + 2\nu\varepsilon$: $$\frac{R}{R_0} \approx (1 + C\varepsilon)(1 + \varepsilon)(1 + 2\nu\varepsilon)$$ **Step 8 — Expand and drop higher-order products of $\varepsilon$ (e.g., $\varepsilon^2$):** $$\frac{R}{R_0} \approx 1 + \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon + C\varepsilon$$ **Step 9 — Subtract 1 to get the fractional change:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R_0} \approx (1 + 2\nu + C)\varepsilon$$ **Step 10 — Define the Gauge Factor:** $$\boxed{\; GF \;\equiv\; \frac{\Delta R / R_0}{\varepsilon} \;=\; 1 + 2\nu + C \;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - The "$1$" comes from pure length change. > - The "$2\nu$" comes from cross-sectional area reduction (Poisson effect). > - The "$C$" is the *piezoresistive term* — small for metals (≈ 0), but **dominant for semiconductors** (can be ±100 to ±200), which is why semiconductor gauges have massive gauge factors. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheatstone Bridge Output for a Single Active Gauge (Quarter Bridge) **Given:** Bridge with $R_1 = R$, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_0$, and $R_1$ changes to $R_1 = R + \Delta R$. **Step 1 — Substitute into the standard bridge equation:** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{R + \Delta R}{(R + \Delta R) + R_0} - \frac{R_0}{R_0 + R_0}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Assume the bridge is initially balanced, so $R = R_0$:** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0 + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Combine over a common denominator:** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{2(R_0 + \Delta R) - (2R_0 + \Delta R)}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the numerator:** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{2R_0 + 2\Delta R - 2R_0 - \Delta R}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}\right] = V_{in}\left[\frac{\Delta R}{2(2R_0 + \Delta R)}\right]$$ **Step 5 — Since $\Delta R \ll R_0$, denominator $\approx 4R_0$:** $$\boxed{\; V_{out} \;\approx\; \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0} \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon \;}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Full-Bridge Output for Adjacent-Arm Differential Gauges **Given:** $R_1 \to R_0 + \Delta R$ (tension gauge), $R_2 \to R_0 - \Delta R$ (compression gauge from Poisson), $R_3 = R_4 = R_0$ unchanged. **Step 1 — Apply the bridge formula:** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Plug in the gauge values:** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{(R_0 + \Delta R) + (R_0 - \Delta R)} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the denominator of the first term (the $\Delta R$ terms cancel!):** $$V_{out} = V_{in}\left[\frac{R_0 + \Delta R}{2R_0} - \frac{1}{2}\right] = V_{in}\left[\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\Delta R}{2R_0} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Result:** $$\boxed{\; V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2R_0} \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{2} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon \;}$$ **Step 5 — For a *true* full bridge with all 4 gauges active (2 in tension, 2 in compression), one can show the output doubles again:** $$\boxed{\; V_{out}^{full} = V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon \;}$$ This is the result used in commercial load cells. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Force-to-Voltage Transfer Function of a Load Cell **Given:** A load cell of capacity $F_{max}$ with rated output $S$ (mV/V) at excitation $V_{ex}$. **Step 1 — For a load $F$ applied, the strain in the spring element is:** $$\varepsilon = k \cdot F$$ where $k$ is the structural design constant (strain per unit force), determined by the geometry and Young's modulus of the spring element. **Step 2 — Substitute into the full-bridge output formula:** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot k \cdot F$$ **Step 3 — Calibrate so that $V_{out}$ at $F = F_{max}$ equals the rated output $S \cdot V_{ex}$:** $$S \cdot V_{ex} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot k \cdot F_{max} \quad \Rightarrow \quad GF \cdot k = \frac{S}{F_{max}}$$ **Step 4 — Therefore, the working equation of a load cell is:** $$\boxed{\; F = \frac{V_{out}}{S \cdot V_{ex}} \cdot F_{max} \;}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Example:** A 100 kN load cell with rated output $S = 2\ \text{mV/V}$ is excited with $V_{ex} = 10\ \text{V}$. The output reads $V_{out} = 12.5\ \text{mV}$. Calculate the applied force. $$F = \frac{12.5\ \text{mV}}{(2\ \text{mV/V})(10\ \text{V})} \cdot 100\ \text{kN} = \frac{12.5}{20} \cdot 100\ \text{kN} = 62.5\ \text{kN}$$ ## 3.5 Sensitivity & Resolution Calculation The **resolution** of a load cell system depends on the noise floor of the readout electronics. For a 24-bit ADC with $\pm 10\ \text{V}$ input range, the LSB is: $$V_{LSB} = \frac{20\ \text{V}}{2^{24}} = \frac{20}{16{,}777{,}216} \approx 1.19\ \mu\text{V}$$ For the 100 kN cell above, the resolution in force is: $$F_{LSB} = \frac{1.19 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{V}}{20\ \text{mV}} \cdot 100\ \text{kN} = 5.95 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kN} \approx 6\ \text{N}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Pitfall Alert:** Many students forget that **load cell output is ratiometric** — the output voltage scales linearly with excitation voltage. If you double $V_{ex}$, you double $V_{out}$ *for the same force*. This is why a stable, low-noise excitation source is critical. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Strain Gauge Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mechanical Specimen
under Load] --> B[Strain Gauge
Bonded to Surface] B --> C[Wheatstone Bridge
Quarter/Half/Full] C --> D[Amplifier
Instrumentation Amp] D --> E[Filter
Low-pass] E --> F[ADC
Analog to Digital] F --> G[Microcontroller / DAQ] G --> H[Display / Computer
Force or Strain Output] I[Excitation Source
5V or 10V DC] --> C I --> D ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Full-Bridge Strain Gauge Wiring Inside a Load Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Excitation VEX["+V excitation
RED wire"] GND["-V excitation
BLACK wire"] end subgraph FullBridge["Wheatstone Full Bridge"] R1["R1 (Tension gauge)
Top-Left"] R2["R2 (Compression)
Top-Right"] R3["R3 (Compression)
Bottom-Left"] R4["R4 (Tension gauge)
Bottom-Right"] end subgraph Output VOP["Vout+
GREEN wire"] VOM["Vout-
WHITE wire"] end VEX --> R1 VEX --> R2 R1 --> VOP R2 --> VOP R1 --> R3 R2 --> R4 R3 --> GND R4 --> GND R3 --> VOM R4 --> VOM ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Load Cell Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart TB L["Applied Load F"] --> LE["Load Cell
Spring Element"] LE --> SG["Strain Gauges
x4 active"] SG --> WB["Wheatstone
Bridge"] EXC["DC Excitation
Source 10V"] --> WB WB --> IA["Instrumentation
Amplifier"] IA --> LPF["Low-Pass
Filter"] LPF --> ADC["24-bit ADC"] ADC --> MC["Microcontroller"] MC --> DISP["LCD / Digital
Display"] MC --> PC["Computer
Data Logging"] CAL["Calibration
Constants stored
in EEPROM"] --> MC ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow — Calibration Procedure for a Load Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Step1: Mount Load Cell
vertically aligned] --> B[Step2: Apply Zero Load
record zero offset] B --> C[Step3: Apply Known
Calibration Weights] C --> D[Step4: Record Output
at each step] D --> E[Step5: Linear Regression
for slope and intercept] E --> F[Step6: Calculate
Sensitivity S] F --> G[Step7: Check
Non-linearity and Hysteresis] G --> H[Step8: Update
Calibration Constants] H --> I[Step9: Lock Settings
and Seal Cell] ``` ## 4.5 Comparison Block — Types of Load Cells ```mermaid flowchart TB LC["Load Cell Types"] LC --> HYD["Hydraulic Load Cell
Pressure-based"] LC --> PNE["Pneumatic Load Cell
Air pressure-based"] LC --> SG["Strain Gauge Load Cell
Most common"] HYD --> HYDuse["Use: Hazardous areas
Very high loads"] PNE --> PNEuse["Use: Clean rooms
Food/pharma"] SG --> SGuse["Use: General purpose
Highest accuracy"] SG --> SGtypes["Subtypes: S-type
Shear-web, Column
Button, Pancake"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mark Scheme Note (3-mark Qs):** Definition carries 1 mark, explanation/diagram 1 mark, example/application 1 mark. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define gauge factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for metallic foil gauges?** **Model Answer:** The **gauge factor (GF)** is defined as the ratio of fractional change in electrical resistance to the mechanical strain that caused it: $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$ For metallic foil gauges, the typical value of gauge factor is **2.0 to 2.2**. **(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark value)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is a load cell? List any two advantages of strain gauge-based load cells.** **Model Answer:** A **load cell** is a force transducer that converts an applied mechanical load into a measurable electrical signal. **(1 mark)** **Advantages:** (any two) **(2 marks)** 1. High accuracy and repeatability 2. Compact size and low cost 3. Linear output over a wide range 4. Wide capacity range (few N to thousands of kN) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two alternatives provided — answer *either* full set. Each sub-part should have a diagram/derivation (3 marks), explanation (2 marks), and conclusion/example (2 marks) for a 7-mark question. --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a bonded metallic strain gauge. Derive the expression for its gauge factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A strain gauge with $R = 350\ \Omega$ and $GF = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel specimen ($E = 200\ \text{GPa}$) subjected to a stress of $140\ \text{MPa}$. Find (i) the strain, (ii) the change in resistance, and (iii) the output voltage if it is connected in a quarter Wheatstone bridge with 10 V excitation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A (a): **Construction (3 marks):** - A bonded metallic strain gauge consists of a **thin grid of resistance wire or etched metal foil** (typically constantan or karma alloy) arranged in a zig-zag (serpentine) pattern. - The grid is **sandwiched between a thin flexible backing** (polyimide or epoxy carrier) and a **protective cover layer**. - Fine lead wires (solder tabs) connect the grid to the external circuit. - The gauge is **bonded to the test specimen** using a special adhesive (cyanoacrylate or epoxy) such that it deforms in unison with the specimen. **Working (2 marks):** - When the specimen is loaded, it experiences strain $\varepsilon$ in the active axis. - This strain is transferred to the gauge through the adhesive. - The wire grid stretches → length $L$ increases, area $A$ decreases → resistance $R$ changes. - This $\Delta R$ is measured using a Wheatstone bridge and converted to strain. **Derivation (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 above — full derivation of $GF = 1 + 2\nu + C$)* #### Model Solution to Q-A (b): **Given:** $R = 350\ \Omega$, $GF = 2.1$, $\sigma = 140\ \text{MPa}$, $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$, $V_{in} = 10\ \text{V}$, quarter bridge. **Step (i) — Calculate strain using Hooke's Law:** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{140 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 7 \times 10^{-4} = 700\ \mu\varepsilon$$ **Step (ii) — Calculate change in resistance:** $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 7 \times 10^{-4} = 1.47 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\Delta R = 1.47 \times 10^{-3} \times 350 = 0.5145\ \Omega$$ **Step (iii) — Output voltage for quarter bridge:** $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{10}{4} \times 2.1 \times 7 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$V_{out} = 2.5 \times 1.47 \times 10^{-3} = 3.675 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 3.675\ \text{mV}$$ **Final Answers:** $\varepsilon = 700\ \mu\varepsilon$; $\Delta R = 0.5145\ \Omega$; $V_{out} = 3.675\ \text{mV}$. **(7 marks: 2+2+3)** --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the working of a Wheatstone bridge. Derive the expression for output voltage when all four arms are active. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A load cell has a rated capacity of 50 kN and rated output of 2.5 mV/V. It is excited with 12 V DC. Find (i) the output voltage at full load, (ii) the output voltage when a force of 18 kN is applied, and (iii) the smallest force change that can be detected if the readout can resolve 0.01 mV. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B (a): **Circuit (2 marks):** Draw the standard Wheatstone bridge with $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, excitation $V_{in}$, and output $V_{out}$ across the midpoints. **Working (2 marks):** When bridge is balanced, $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$, $V_{out} = 0$. Strain changes the resistances, unbalancing the bridge and producing $V_{out}$ proportional to strain. **Derivation (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.3 — full bridge derivation)*: $$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$ #### Model Solution to Q-B (b): **Given:** $F_{max} = 50\ \text{kN}$, $S = 2.5\ \text{mV/V}$, $V_{ex} = 12\ \text{V}$. **Step (i) — Output at full load:** $$V_{out, max} = S \cdot V_{ex} = 2.5 \times 12 = 30\ \text{mV}$$ **Step (ii) — Output at 18 kN (linearity assumed):** $$V_{out} = \frac{18}{50} \times 30\ \text{mV} = 0.36 \times 30 = 10.8\ \text{mV}$$ **Step (iii) — Force resolution at 0.01 mV resolution:** $$\Delta F = \frac{0.01\ \text{mV}}{30\ \text{mV}} \times 50\ \text{kN} = \frac{0.01}{30} \times 50\ \text{kN} = 0.0167\ \text{kN} \approx 16.7\ \text{N}$$ **Final Answers:** $V_{out,max} = 30\ \text{mV}$; $V_{out}(18\ \text{kN}) = 10.8\ \text{mV}$; $\Delta F_{min} = 16.7\ \text{N}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide by 4** in a quarter-bridge formula — most common error. Marks lost: 1–2. > 2. **Confusing mV/V with mV** — "rated output 2.0 mV/V" is a *sensitivity*, not an absolute output. Always multiply by excitation voltage. > 3. **Using wrong Poisson sign** in full-bridge derivation — gauges on opposite arms of the bridge *subtract*; gauges on adjacent arms *add*. Marks lost: 1. > 4. **Not stating units** for $\varepsilon$ (microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$ vs dimensionless) — penalised in technical communication marks. > 5. **Writing gauge factor formula as $GF = 1 + 2\nu$** — incomplete! Must include the piezoresistive term $C$ for semiconductor gauges. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] A **strain gauge** is a resistive sensor that changes resistance proportionally to mechanical strain. - [x] **Gauge factor** $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ = **2.0 (typical, metallic)** or **±100 to ±200 (semiconductor)**. - [x] **Quarter-bridge** output: $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$. - [x] **Full-bridge** output: $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ — gives 4× the sensitivity of quarter bridge. - [x] **Load cell** = spring element + strain gauges in a Wheatstone bridge. Rated output typically **2 to 3 mV/V**. - [x] **Force equation:** $F = \dfrac{V_{out}}{S \cdot V_{ex}} \cdot F_{max}$. - [x] Load cell types: **Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Strain Gauge (S-type, shear-web, button, pancake, column)**. - [x] Key specifications: **Capacity, Rated Output, Non-linearity, Hysteresis, Creep, Temperature effects, Safe/Ultimate Overload**. - [x] Always calibrate load cells with **known certified weights**; use **6-wire connection** for long cables to eliminate lead-resistance errors. - [x] **Temperature compensation** is automatic in a full-bridge configuration because all four gauges experience identical thermal expansion. - [x] Strain gauges are **directional** — they measure strain only along the **active (grid) axis**; transverse strain is much less sensitive. - [x] Remember to apply **gauge factor correction** in software if the gauge GF differs from the default (2.0). - [x] The **backing material, adhesive type, and curing procedure** critically affect gauge performance — never re-bond a used gauge.

topics.title: Bend & re-bend test on MS rods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bend & re-bend test on MS rods

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Bend and Re-bend Test** is a destructive mechanical test performed on **Mild Steel (MS) rods / Thermo-Mechanically Treated (TMT) bars** to evaluate their **ductility, malleability, and resistance to crack initiation** when subjected to plastic deformation. The test is conducted as per the **Indian Standard code IS 1599:2012** (Method for Bend Test) and **IS 1786:2008** (High Strength Deformed Steel Bars and Wires for Concrete Reinforcement — Specification).

The test sequence is twofold:
1. **Bend Test:** The MS rod is bent through an angle of **180°** around a mandrel of specified diameter, and the outer surface of the bend is visually inspected for cracks, splits, or fractures.
2. **Re-bend Test:** The previously bent specimen is artificially aged (heated to 100 °C for 30–60 minutes, then cooled), and re-bent back through an angle of **45°** in the opposite direction. The specimen is again inspected for cracks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The re-bend test is the **hallmark quality-control test** for rebars in RCC construction. It simulates the cold-working stresses (straightening on-site, bending in reinforcement cages) that bars undergo before being embedded in concrete. Failure in this test directly disqualifies the lot for structural use.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a piece of **steel wire coat hanger** in your hand. If you bend it sharply at a point, the outer surface stretches and the inner surface compresses. Now, can you bend it back straight without it breaking? If the steel is ductile, yes — if brittle, it snaps.

The **bend test** is like bending a coat hanger fully into a U-shape. A good rebar will bend cleanly without cracking. The **re-bend test** is the harsher sequel — taking that U-shaped hanger, ageing it (like leaving it in the hot sun for a while, which simulates long-term stress relief), and then partially unbending it. Only highly ductile steel survives both rounds.

**Real-world engineering utility:** Reinforcement bars in columns, beams, and slabs must be bent into stirrups, hooks, and crank bars at construction sites. If a bar is brittle or has surface defects, it will crack during bending, compromising the structural integrity. This test is the on-site guarantee of that ductility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants / metrics used in this test (highlighted per protocol):**
> - **Mandrel diameter (for bars ≤ 10 mm): 2 × d** (where *d* = nominal bar diameter)
> - **Mandrel diameter (for bars > 10 mm up to 16 mm): 3 × d**
> - **Mandrel diameter (for bars > 16 mm): 4 × d**
> - **First bend angle: 180°**
> - **Re-bend angle: 45°** (in the reverse direction)
> - **Ageing temperature: 100 °C** for **30–60 minutes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background

### 2.1.1 Why Ductility Matters in MS Rods
Mild steel is a **ferrite-pearlite** aggregate with a body-centered cubic (BCC) iron matrix. Its ductility arises from **dislocation glide** along slip planes. When a bar is bent, the outer fibers experience **tensile plastic strain**, while the inner fibers undergo **compressive plastic strain**. The strain at the outermost fiber of a bent rod of diameter *d* bent around a mandrel of diameter *D* is given by the **bending strain formula**:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2D}{d}}$$

- For a larger mandrel, strain is lower → easier to pass.
- For a smaller mandrel, strain is higher → cracks appear sooner.
- The minimum acceptable strain correlates with the **percentage elongation** requirement of the bar (typically **≥ 14.5% for Fe 415**, **≥ 12.5% for Fe 500** as per IS 1786).

### 2.1.2 Metallurgical Significance of the Test
The bend test exposes:
- **Surface defects** (seams, laps, rolling marks) — these open up as cracks on the tension face.
- **Inclusions** (slag, oxides) — they act as stress concentrators.
- **Insufficient ductility** — if the bar has excessive carbon or improper heat treatment.
- **Strain-ageing effects** — captured specifically by the *re-bend* test, where interstitial nitrogen and carbon atoms pin dislocations after the first bend and ageing, making the second bend more demanding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Re-bend Test is Unique to Rebars:** No other standard mechanical test for steel captures **strain-ageing embrittlement**. This is why IS 1786 makes it mandatory for high-strength deformed bars used in reinforced concrete.

## 2.2 Operational Procedure — Structured Logic Steps

### Phase A: Specimen Preparation
1. Select a **representative MS rod sample** of length approximately **300 mm to 500 mm**.
2. Ensure the bar is **straight** (use a straightening roll or mallet if mildly crooked — do not hammer the test portion).
3. Measure and record the **nominal diameter *d*** at three points using a vernier caliper (accuracy **0.01 mm**). Average the readings.
4. Visually inspect for pre-existing surface defects; reject the specimen if any exist.

### Phase B: First Bend (180°)
5. Select the **mandrel** of appropriate diameter per IS 1786 (2*d*, 3*d*, or 4*d* depending on bar size).
6. Position the specimen in the **bending jig** of a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) or a **hydraulic bending machine**.
7. Apply a **continuous, smooth, and uniform load** — no impact. Bend the bar through **180°** at a steady rate (typical rate: **1 rad/s** or about 60°/s).
8. Inspect the **outer (tension) surface** of the bend for **visible cracks, splits, or fractures** using a magnifying glass (10× magnification recommended).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pass Criterion (Bend Test):** No visible crack on the outer surface of the bend after 180°.

### Phase C: Artificial Ageing
9. Place the bent specimen in a **hot air oven** or **boiling water bath** at **100 °C** for **30 minutes** (for bar sizes ≤ 10 mm) or **60 minutes** (for larger bars).
10. Allow it to **cool naturally to room temperature** in still air. **Do not quench.**

### Phase D: Re-bend (45°)
11. Reposition the aged specimen in the bending jig with the **previously bent end as the new pivot**.
12. Bend the specimen back through **45°** in the **opposite direction** (so total deflection from the post-aged position is 45°).
13. Re-inspect the bend region (now the **inner surface of the original bend** has become the new **outer tension surface**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pass Criterion (Re-bend Test):** No visible crack or fracture on the bent surface after the 45° reverse bend.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Standard Value** | **Unit** | **Code Reference** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal bar diameter | $d$ | Measured using vernier caliper | mm | IS 1786 |
| Mandrel diameter (≤ 10 mm bars) | $D$ | $D = 2d$ | mm | IS 1786 Cl. 6.3.2 |
| Mandrel diameter (10–16 mm bars) | $D$ | $D = 3d$ | mm | IS 1786 Cl. 6.3.2 |
| Mandrel diameter (> 16 mm bars) | $D$ | $D = 4d$ | mm | IS 1786 Cl. 6.3.2 |
| First bend angle | $\theta_1$ | $180°$ | degrees | IS 1599 |
| Re-bend angle | $\theta_2$ | $45°$ | degrees | IS 1786 |
| Ageing temperature | $T$ | $100$ | °C | IS 1786 |
| Ageing duration | $t$ | $30$ (small bars) or $60$ (large bars) | min | IS 1786 |
| Bending strain at outer fiber | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2D}{d}}$ | dimensionless | Mechanics of Materials |
| Strain ratio for $D = 2d$ | $\varepsilon$ | $1/5 = 0.20$ or $20\%$ | dimensionless | Derived |
| Strain ratio for $D = 3d$ | $\varepsilon$ | $1/7 \approx 14.3\%$ | dimensionless | Derived |
| Strain ratio for $D = 4d$ | $\varepsilon$ | $1/9 \approx 11.1\%$ | dimensionless | Derived |
| Magnification for inspection | $M$ | $10\times$ (recommended) | — | Lab convention |
| Specimen length (typical) | $L$ | $300$ to $500$ | mm | IS 1599 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Always record the **mandrel diameter actually used**, the **bar diameter**, the **ageing time**, and **photographs** of the bent specimens (before and after each bend) as per KTU lab record conventions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Strain at the Outer Fiber

**Given:** A cylindrical bar of diameter *d* is bent around a cylindrical mandrel of diameter *D*. Find the **true plastic strain at the outermost fiber** of the bar.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the neutral axis:**

When the bar is bent around the mandrel, the **neutral axis** of the bent bar lies at the mid-thickness of the bar, i.e., at a radial distance of *d/2* from the bar's own centerline. The total radius of curvature of the neutral axis, measured from the **center of the mandrel**, is:

$$R_{n} = \dfrac{D}{2} + \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{D + d}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Arc length of the outermost fiber:**

For a bend through an angle $\theta$ (in radians), the arc length of the outermost fiber (which is at radius $R_{n} + d/2$ from the mandrel center) is:

$$L_{\text{outer}} = \left(R_{n} + \dfrac{d}{2}\right)\theta = \left(\dfrac{D + d}{2} + \dfrac{d}{2}\right)\theta = \left(\dfrac{D + 2d}{2}\right)\theta$$

**Step 3 — Original (unstressed) length of the outer fiber:**

The original length of the outer fiber segment (before bending) equals the neutral axis arc length (since fibers are inextensible at first order in pure bending):

$$L_{0} = R_{n}\,\theta = \left(\dfrac{D + d}{2}\right)\theta$$

**Step 4 — Engineering strain calculation:**

By definition of engineering (nominal) strain:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_{\text{outer}} - L_{0}}{L_{0}} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{D + 2d}{2}\right)\theta - \left(\dfrac{D + d}{2}\right)\theta}{\left(\dfrac{D + d}{2}\right)\theta}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification:**

The factor $\theta$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\dfrac{D + 2d}{2} - \dfrac{D + d}{2}}{\dfrac{D + d}{2}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{(D + 2d) - (D + d)}{2}}{\dfrac{D + d}{2}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d}{2}}{\dfrac{D + d}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{d}{D + d}}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form (using the IS-code-style variable substitution):**

The above is mathematically equivalent to the form seen in many lab manuals:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{D}{d}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2D}{2d}} \quad \text{(no, let's re-express correctly)}$$

Re-expressing with the *ratio of mandrel diameter to bar diameter*:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{d}{D + d} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{D}{d} + 1}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluations for standard mandrel ratios:**

Case 1: $D = 2d$ (small bars)

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \;\; (33.3\%)$$

Case 2: $D = 3d$ (medium bars)

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{3 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 \;\; (25.0\%)$$

Case 3: $D = 4d$ (large bars)

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{4 + 1} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.20 \;\; (20.0\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the strain decreases with mandrel size:** A larger mandrel "stretches" the outer fiber over a longer arc, reducing the localized strain. Smaller mandrels sharply curve the bar, demanding greater local ductility. The mandated mandrel-to-bar ratio therefore acts as a built-in ductility threshold.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Pass / Fail Decision Logic

| **Test Phase** | **Observation** | **Verdict** | **Engineering Implication** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visual inspection of unstressed bar | No surface defects | Sample acceptable | Proceed |
| Visual inspection of unstressed bar | Visible seams, rust pits, or rolling marks | Sample rejected | Replace specimen |
| After 180° bend | Smooth bend, no cracks, no splits | **PASS — Bend Test** | Bar is ductile enough for site bending |
| After 180° bend | Visible crack on outer surface | **FAIL — Bend Test** | Material lacks ductility; lot rejected |
| After ageing (100 °C / 30–60 min) | No visible change | Proceed to re-bend | Strain-ageing has occurred internally |
| After 45° re-bend | No crack, no split | **PASS — Re-bend Test** | Bar is suitable for RCC reinforcement |
| After 45° re-bend | Visible crack or fracture | **FAIL — Re-bend Test** | Strain-ageing embrittlement; lot rejected |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** An MS bar of nominal diameter **16 mm** is subjected to a bend and re-bend test. Determine: (a) the **mandrel diameter** to be used, (b) the **bending strain** at the outer fiber, and (c) the **expected percentage elongation** of the bar if it just passes the test.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Mandrel diameter:**

For a 16 mm bar, IS 1786 Cl. 6.3.2 specifies $D = 3d$:

$$D = 3 \times 16 = 48 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b) — Bending strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{d}{D + d} = \dfrac{16}{48 + 16} = \dfrac{16}{64} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Part (c) — Expected minimum elongation:**

For a bar to pass the bend test, its **uniform elongation capacity** must be at least equal to the bending strain. Hence the bar must have:

$$\% \text{Elongation} \geq 25\%$$

Since IS 1786 requires only **14.5% for Fe 415** and **12.5% for Fe 500**, this seems to indicate that *uniform* elongation (not total elongation) is the relevant metric. In practice, bend tests are more lenient than direct tensile elongation because the strained region is localized and the bar can strain-harden further. This is why the IS code mandates the bend test as a **complementary** check, not a replacement for the tensile test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Test Apparatus and Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: MS Rod Sample 300-500 mm] --> B[Measure Bar Diameter d using Vernier Caliper]
    B --> C{Select Mandrel as per IS 1786}
    C -->|d <= 10 mm| D1[Mandrel D = 2d]
    C -->|10 < d <= 16 mm| D2[Mandrel D = 3d]
    C -->|d > 16 mm| D3[Mandrel D = 4d]
    D1 --> E[Mount Specimen in Bending Jig of UTM]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Smooth Load Bend to 180 deg]
    F --> G{Visual Inspection 10x Magnification}
    G -->|Crack Visible| H1[FAIL Bend Test Reject Lot]
    G -->|No Crack| H2[PASS Bend Test Proceed]
    H2 --> I[Age in Oven at 100 deg C for 30 to 60 min]
    I --> J[Cool to Room Temp Still Air No Quench]
    J --> K[Reposition in Bending Jig]
    K --> L[Re-bend 45 deg in Opposite Direction]
    L --> M{Visual Inspection 10x Magnification}
    M -->|Crack Visible| N1[FAIL Re-bend Test Reject Lot]
    M -->|No Crack| N2[PASS Re-bend Test Bar Approved for RCC]
    H1 --> Z[End]
    N1 --> Z
    N2 --> Z
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of the Bending Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LoadingSystem[Loading System]
        LS1[Hydraulic Actuator] --> LS2[Load Cell]
        LS2 --> LS3[Pressure Gauge]
    end
    subgraph BendingJig[Bending Jig Assembly]
        BJ1[Fixed Mandrel Holder] --> BJ2[Mandrel Pin D]
        BJ2 --> BJ3[Specimen Clamp]
        BJ3 --> BJ4[Reaction Anvil]
    end
    subgraph InspectionStation[Inspection Station]
        IS1[10x Magnifying Lens] --> IS2[Crack Detection]
        IS2 --> IS3[Photographic Record]
    end
    subgraph AgeingUnit[Ageing Unit]
        AU1[Hot Air Oven 100 deg C] --> AU2[Timer 30 to 60 min]
        AU2 --> AU3[Cooling Rack]
    end
    LoadingSystem --> BendingJig
    BendingJig --> InspectionStation
    InspectionStation --> AgeingUnit
    AgeingUnit --> BendingJig
    BendingJig --> InspectionStation
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Geometry of Bent Bar Showing Strain Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Geometry[Bent Bar Geometry]
        G1[Center of Mandrel] --- G2[Inner Surface of Bar Compressed]
        G2 --- G3[Neutral Axis at d by 2 from bar center]
        G3 --- G4[Outer Surface of Bar Tensile Strained]
        G4 --- G5[Radius to outer fiber = D by 2 + d]
        G1 --- G5
    end
    subgraph StrainMap[Strain Distribution]
        S1[Inner Fiber: Max Compression] --> S2[Neutral Axis: Zero Strain]
        S2 --> S3[Outer Fiber: Max Tension epsilon = d over D plus d]
    end
    Geometry --> StrainMap
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define the bend and re-bend test on MS rods. State the relevant Indian Standard code.**

**Model Answer:**

The **bend test** is a ductility test in which an MS rod is bent through **180°** around a mandrel of prescribed diameter (2*d*, 3*d*, or 4*d* depending on bar size) and the outer bend surface is inspected for cracks.

The **re-bend test** is performed subsequently on the same specimen after **artificial ageing at 100 °C for 30–60 minutes**. The specimen is bent back through **45°** in the reverse direction and re-inspected. It specifically assesses **strain-ageing embrittlement**.

**Relevant IS codes:**
- **IS 1599:2012** — Method for Bend Test
- **IS 1786:2008** — Specification for high-strength deformed steel bars (mandates both tests)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is the purpose of the artificial ageing step in the re-bend test? Why is it done at 100 °C?**

**Model Answer:**

The **artificial ageing** step simulates the **long-term strain-ageing effect** that the reinforcement bar will experience over its service life. During ageing, **interstitial nitrogen and carbon atoms** in the BCC iron lattice migrate to **dislocation cores** generated by the first 180° bend, thereby **pinning** these dislocations. This makes the steel **harder but more brittle**, which is precisely the worst-case condition the bar may face on site.

The temperature of **100 °C** (boiling water temperature) is chosen because:
1. It is **easily reproducible** in a standard laboratory without specialized equipment.
2. It is **high enough to accelerate diffusion** of C and N atoms (which would otherwise take months/years at room temperature).
3. It is **below the tempering range** of mild steel, so no metallurgical phase change occurs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the procedure for conducting the **bend and re-bend test on a 12 mm diameter MS rod** as per IS 1786. List the apparatus required and explain the acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 20 mm diameter TMT bar is subjected to a bend test. Calculate the **bending strain at the outermost fiber** when the bar is bent around the prescribed mandrel. Comment on whether the bar would have passed if its percentage elongation in a tensile test were **12%**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** or **hydraulic press** with a bending attachment
2. **Bending jig** with interchangeable mandrels (pins) of 2*d*, 3*d*, 4*d*
3. **Vernier caliper** (0.01 mm accuracy)
4. **Hot air oven** (capable of 100 °C ± 5 °C)
5. **10× magnifying glass**
6. **Straightening mallet / roll** (pre-conditioning)
7. **Stopwatch** and **temperature indicator**

**Procedure (stepwise):**

1. **Specimen preparation:** Cut a 12 mm MS rod to **300–500 mm** length. Measure diameter at three points using vernier caliper and record the mean as $d = 12$ mm. Straighten if necessary.
2. **Mandrel selection:** Per IS 1786, for 12 mm bar: $D = 3d = 36$ mm.
3. **First bend (180°):** Mount the bar in the bending jig with the 36 mm mandrel. Apply load gradually and continuously. Bend through **180°** in a steady motion (avoid impact).
4. **Inspection:** Visually examine the **outer (tension) surface** of the bend using 10× magnification. **Pass criterion:** No visible cracks, splits, or fractures.
5. **Ageing:** Place the bent specimen in a preheated oven at **100 °C** for **60 minutes** (since bar > 10 mm).
6. **Cooling:** Remove and allow to cool to room temperature in still air. **Do not quench** in water.
7. **Re-bend (45°):** Reposition the specimen in the jig with the previously bent end as the pivot. Bend back through **45°** in the reverse direction.
8. **Final inspection:** Examine the new outer surface (formerly the inner side) for cracks. **Pass criterion:** No visible cracks.

**Acceptance criteria (summarized):**
- No cracks after 180° bend.
- No cracks after 45° re-bend.
- The lot is accepted for RCC use only if **both** criteria are met.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $d = 20$ mm (so $D = 4d = 80$ mm per IS 1786 for bars > 16 mm).

**Step 1: Bending strain calculation**

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{d}{D + d} = \dfrac{20}{80 + 20} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Step 2: Required ductility**

For the bar to just pass the bend test without cracking, the **outer fiber strain (20%)** must be accommodated by the material's ductility. The percentage elongation from a tensile test is a related but not identical measure — the **localized true strain** at the bend must exceed 20%.

**Step 3: Comparison with 12% elongation**

A bar with only **12% total elongation** in a tensile test typically has uniform elongation of about **8–9%** (the rest being post-necking). The 20% bending strain far exceeds this uniform elongation capacity.

**Conclusion:** The bar would **NOT pass** the bend test. The 12% elongation is insufficient to absorb the 20% outer-fiber strain without localized cracking. This illustrates why the **bend test is a stricter ductility screen** than a basic elongation check for rebars.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A(a):**
> Students frequently forget to mention (a) the **ageing duration depends on bar size** (30 min for ≤ 10 mm, 60 min for > 10 mm), and (b) the specimen must be **cooled in still air, not quenched**. Quenching introduces additional thermal stresses that mask the true strain-ageing effect. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this omission.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **metallurgical reasons** why a rebar must pass both the bend and re-bend tests. Discuss what types of defects the test can detect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the bend test mandrel specifications of IS 1786 for different bar sizes. For a 10 mm bar, calculate the strain at the outer fiber when bent over (i) 2*d* mandrel and (ii) 3*d* mandrel. What conclusion do you draw? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Metallurgical reasons:**

Mild steel and TMT bars contain **ferrite (α-Fe, BCC)** as the primary phase with dispersed **pearlite** (lamellar Fe + Fe₃C). Ductility in ferrite arises from **dislocation glide** on {110} and {112} planes.

1. **First bend (180°):** Generates a high density of **mobile dislocations** and **deformation twins** at the outer fibers. Mobile dislocations allow continued plastic flow without fracture.
2. **Artificial ageing:** At 100 °C, **interstitial C and N atoms** (dissolved in the ferrite lattice during steelmaking) diffuse to dislocation cores and form **Cottrell atmospheres**, pinning the dislocations. This phenomenon is called **strain ageing**.
3. **Re-bend (45°):** New dislocations must be generated or unpinned to accommodate the additional strain. If the original dislocation network is too strongly pinned, **stress concentration** builds up at grain boundaries and inclusions, nucleating **microcracks** that coalesce into visible fractures.

**Defects detected:**

| **Defect Type** | **Origin** | **Manifestation in Test** |
|---|---|---|
| Seams, laps, scabs | Rolling mill surface defects | Open into cracks on outer bend |
| Excessive carbon | Improper composition | Brittle fracture with little deformation |
| Inadequate heat treatment (TMT) | Quench/temper imbalance | Strain-ageing cracks at re-bend |
| Inclusions (slag, MnS) | Deoxidation / raw material | Cracks nucleating at inclusion sites |
| Hydrogen embrittlement | Improper cooling or pickling | Delayed cracking in re-bend |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Mandrel specifications (IS 1786 Cl. 6.3.2):**

| **Bar diameter range** | **Mandrel diameter $D$** |
|---|---|
| $d \leq 10$ mm | $2d$ |
| $10 < d \leq 16$ mm | $3d$ |
| $d > 16$ mm | $4d$ |

**Given:** $d = 10$ mm

**Case (i): $D = 2d = 20$ mm**

$$\varepsilon_{1} = \dfrac{d}{D + d} = \dfrac{10}{20 + 10} = \dfrac{10}{30} = 0.333 = 33.3\%$$

**Case (ii): $D = 3d = 30$ mm**

$$\varepsilon_{2} = \dfrac{d}{D + d} = \dfrac{10}{30 + 10} = \dfrac{10}{40} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Conclusion:**

The outer-fiber strain **decreases by 8.3 percentage points** (from 33.3% to 25%) when the mandrel is changed from $2d$ to $3d$. This demonstrates that the **mandrel-to-bar ratio is the single most important parameter controlling test severity**. IS 1786 deliberately mandates progressively larger mandrels for larger bars because:
1. Larger bars are inherently harder to bend sharply.
2. The strain gradient scales inversely with bar size for a fixed mandrel ratio.
3. The code balances **test practicality** (avoiding breakage of large bars at the test lab) against **quality assurance** (ensuring minimum ductility).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> A common error is using $D = 2d$ for *all* bar sizes. The IS 1786 specification is **size-dependent**, and using the wrong mandrel will produce an invalid result. Another common mistake is computing the strain as $d/D$ (without the additive $d$ in the denominator); this gives incorrect values. The correct formula is $\varepsilon = d/(D + d)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bend & Re-bend Test on MS Rods**

- [x] **Bend test angle:** 180°; **Re-bend angle:** 45°
- [x] **Mandrel diameter:** $2d$ for $d \leq 10$ mm; $3d$ for $10 < d \leq 16$ mm; $4d$ for $d > 16$ mm
- [x] **Ageing:** 100 °C for **30 min** (small bars) or **60 min** (large bars)
- [x] **Cooling:** Still air, **never quench**
- [x] **Inspection magnification:** 10× recommended
- [x] **Strain formula:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{d}{D + d} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (D/d)}$
- [x] **IS Codes:** IS 1599:2012 (bend test method); IS 1786:2008 (rebar specification)
- [x] **Pass criterion:** No visible cracks on outer bend surface at **both** stages
- [x] **Metallurgical purpose of re-bend:** Detects **strain-ageing embrittlement** due to C/N pinning of dislocations
- [x] **Why 100 °C?** Accelerates C/N diffusion; below tempering range; reproducible
- [x] **Common defects detected:** Seams, laps, inclusions, brittle ferrite, poor TMT treatment
- [x] **Specimen length:** 300–500 mm (typical)
- [x] **Loading:** Smooth and continuous, **no impact**
- [x] **Record keeping:** Photograph both bends; record $d$, $D$, ageing time, ambient temperature
- [x] **Lot rejection:** Even **one** failed specimen → entire lot rejected for RCC use
- [x] **Engineering significance:** Simulates on-site bar bending (stirrups, hooks, crank bars)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tensile behaviour of polymeric membranes, textiles, fibres, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tensile behaviour of polymeric membranes, textiles, fibres, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Tensile behaviour** refers to the mechanical response of a material when subjected to a uniaxial tensile (pulling) load until failure. For **polymeric membranes, textiles, and fibres**, this behaviour is evaluated through standardized uniaxial tensile tests that record the **load–elongation response** and derive the **stress–strain curve**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> The *tensile behaviour* of a polymeric membrane, textile, or fibre is the characteristic stress–strain relationship observed when a specimen of standardized geometry is stretched at a constant crosshead speed along its principal axis, from which key mechanical descriptors — **tensile strength (σₘ)**, **Young's modulus (E)**, **elongation at break (εᵦ)**, and **toughness (Uₜ)** — are obtained.

### Why Tensile Testing Matters for These Materials

Polymeric membranes (films), textiles (woven/knitted fabrics), and fibres (filaments) differ fundamentally from bulk metals and ceramics in their tensile response:

| Material Class | Structural Nature | Typical Tensile Character |
|---|---|---|
| Polymeric Films (membranes) | 2-D continuous, oriented/semi-crystalline | Anisotropic, strain-rate sensitive |
| Textiles (woven/knitted) | 2-D interlaced yarn network | Highly non-linear due to yarn crimp |
| Fibres (filaments) | 1-D, highly oriented polymer chains | Strong, stiff along the axis, very weak transverse |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Rubber Band vs. The Fishing Line"**
>
> Imagine three everyday objects: a **rubber band** (elastomer film), a **cotton T-shirt strip** (textile), and a **nylon fishing line** (fibre).
>
> - The **rubber band** stretches enormously with little force, then snaps — this is a *low-modulus, high-elongation* film.
> - The **T-shirt strip** feels floppy at first (yarns un-crimping), then suddenly resists strongly, and finally tears — this *J-shaped* curve is characteristic of textiles.
> - The **fishing line** resists strongly from the start and stretches only a little before breaking — a *high-modulus, low-elongation* fibre response.
>
> The **tensile test** is simply the laboratory version of pulling these until they break, while a computer plots the force–extension curve.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standardized)

- **Tensile Stress (σ):** Force per unit original cross-sectional area  
  $$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$  
  Units: **MPa** or **N/mm²** (for fibres often **cN/tex** or **GPa**).

- **Engineering Strain (ε):** Change in length per unit original gauge length  
  $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$  
  Units: **dimensionless** or expressed as **%**.

- **Gauge Length (L₀):** The initial distance between the jaws/grip marks. For fibres: **20 mm, 50 mm, or 500 mm** as per standards.

- **Crosshead Speed (v):** Rate of jaw separation. Typical ranges:
  - Films: 1–500 mm/min
  - Textiles: 20–300 mm/min (as per ASTM D5035, ISO 13934-1)
  - Fibres: 1–500 mm/min (ASTM D3822)

- **Tensile Modulus (Young's Modulus, E):** Initial slope of the σ–ε curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Specimen Geometries (KTU 2024 Lab Standard):**
> - **Films/Membranes:** Dumbbell (Type IV/V) or rectangular strip
> - **Textiles:** Rectangular strip, often 50 mm wide × 200 mm gauge
> - **Fibres:** Single filament gripped by pneumatic or capstan grips
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — What Happens Microscopically?

When a polymeric membrane, textile, or fibre is loaded in tension, three sequential molecular mechanisms occur:

### Step 1 — Elastic Deformation (Reversible)
- Bond stretching and bond-angle distortion along the polymer backbone.
- For **semi-crystalline polymers** (PET, Nylon), the **amorphous tie molecules** extend first, while crystalline regions act as rigid crosslinks.
- **Mathematical signature:** Linear σ–ε region, slope = **E (Young's Modulus)**.

### Step 2 — Yielding and Plastic Flow (Irreversible, mostly)
- **Crazing** (in glassy polymers: PS, PMMA): formation of micro-voids bridged by fibrils.
- **Shear yielding** (in semi-crystalline polymers: PE, PP): lamellar rotation, chain slippage.
- **Textile-specific:** Yarn uncrimping, yarn straightening, and inter-yarn friction mobilization.
- **Fibre-specific:** Necking along the gauge length, axial alignment of microfibrils.

### Step 3 — Strain Hardening and Fracture
- Molecular chains align along the load direction → **strain-induced orientation hardening**.
- Defect nucleation (voids, microcracks) leads to **failure** at ε = εᵦ (elongation at break).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Textiles show a *concave-up* (J-shaped) curve because the **crimp-interchange** mechanism dominates initially. Films of semi-crystalline polymers show a *yield point* followed by *neck propagation* — analogous to a cold-drawing process.

## 2.2 Stress–Strain Curve Features (Master Reference)

| Region | Description | Physical Meaning | Typical Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OA** | Initial elastic region | Bond stretching | Linear, slope = E |
| **AB** | Yield region (films) | Crazing/shear yielding | Peak or plateau (σᵧ) |
| **BC** | Plastic flow / necking | Chain alignment, yarn straightening | Strain softening/hardening |
| **CD** | Strain hardening | Molecular orientation | σ rises steeply |
| **DE** | Fracture point | Crack propagation | (σᵦ, εᵦ) = breaking point |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | MPa | F = instantaneous load |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | % or dimensionless | — |
| 3 | True Stress | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$ | MPa | Assumes constant volume |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$ | — | For large deformations |
| 5 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F L_0}{A_0 \Delta L}$ | MPa | Slope of initial linear region |
| 6 | Toughness (Modulus of Resilience + Plastic) | $U_t = \displaystyle\int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | MJ/m³ | Area under σ–ε curve |
| 7 | Specific Stress (Fibres) | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{F}{\rho A_0}$ | N/tex, cN/dtex | ρ = linear density |
| 8 | Tenacity (Fibres) | $T = \dfrac{F_{break}}{\text{tex}}$ | cN/tex, gf/denier | Textile/fibre convention |
| 9 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Reported with rupture load |
| 10 | Initial Modulus (Fibres) | $E_i = \dfrac{\Delta F / tex}{\Delta \varepsilon}$ | cN/tex per % | At ε = 0.5% to 1% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Conventions:**
> - Films/Membranes: report in **MPa** and **%**.
> - Fibres: report in **cN/tex** (or gf/denier) and **%**.
> - Textiles: report in **N** (force) and **%** elongation, OR strip strength in **N/cm** width.

## 2.4 Standards Referenced in KTU Lab Manuals

| Standard | Scope | Specimen |
|---|---|---|
| **ASTM D882** | Thin plastic sheeting / films | Rectangular film strip |
| **ASTM D5035** | Textile fabric strip test | 50 mm × 200 mm strip |
| **ASTM D3822** | Single-fibre tensile test | Single filament |
| **ISO 13934-1** | Textile strip method | Strip tensile |
| **ISO 527-3** | Plastics films/sheets | Dumbbell |
| **ASTM D3039** | Polymer matrix composites | Not for neat films (composite specific) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Membrane engineering (filtration, packaging, roofing):** ensures films resist in-service tensile and tear loads.
- **Geotextiles (civil engineering):** tensile strength controls slope stabilization and road separation performance.
- **Ballistic fibres (Kevlar, UHMWPE):** tenacity directly translates to impact energy absorption.
- **Smart textiles / e-textiles:** modulus matching between fabric and embedded electronics is critical.
- **Biomedical membranes (PTFE grafts, surgical meshes):** must mimic tissue compliance under pulsatile loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Engineering Stress vs. True Stress Conversion

**Starting Point:** Engineering stress assumes the cross-sectional area remains constant at A₀. True stress accounts for the reduction in area as the material extends.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{true} &= \frac{F}{A} \\
\text{Assuming constant volume:}\quad A \cdot L &= A_0 \cdot L_0 \\
\Rightarrow A &= \frac{A_0 L_0}{L} = \frac{A_0}{1+\varepsilon} \\
\therefore \sigma_{true} &= \frac{F \cdot (1+\varepsilon)}{A_0} = \sigma (1+\varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The material thins as it stretches. For a polymer film reaching ε = 1.0 (100% elongation), the true stress is **twice** the engineering stress. For a textile undergoing yarn-straightening, the correction is minor because area change is small.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — True Strain (Hencky / Logarithmic Strain)

**Starting Point:** For finite (large) deformations, incremental strain is summed logarithmically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\varepsilon_{true} &= \frac{dL}{L} \\
\varepsilon_{true} &= \int_{L_0}^{L_b} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_b}{L_0}\right) \\
&= \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0}\right) \\
&= \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Logarithmic strain correctly accumulates the deformation history. For a rubbery film at ε_eng = 5.0, true strain is ln(6) ≈ 1.79.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Young's Modulus from the Linear Region

**Starting Point:** Hooke's Law applied to the initial linear portion of the σ–ε curve.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{(in linear region)} \\
E &= \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L/L_0} = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This derivation is the basis for the **chord modulus** (between two strain points) and the **initial modulus** (slope near ε = 0) used for fibres.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Toughness from the Stress–Strain Curve

**Starting Point:** Toughness is the energy per unit volume absorbed up to fracture, equal to the area under the σ–ε curve.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_t &= \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon \\
\text{Approximation by trapezoidal rule:}\quad U_t &\approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(\sigma_i + \sigma_{i+1})}{2} \cdot (\varepsilon_{i+1} - \varepsilon_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Modern tensile testing software computes this area digitally. For a KTU lab report, this integral is often computed via Simpson's rule or numerical summation in Excel.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Tenacity and Specific Stress for Fibres

**Starting Point:** A fibre's tensile response is conventionally expressed per unit linear density (tex) rather than per unit area, because measuring A of a single filament is impractical.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\ \text{(Tenacity, cN/tex)} &= \frac{F_{break}\ \text{(cN)}}{\text{Linear Density (tex)}} \\
\text{where tex} &= \frac{\text{mass (g)}}{1000\ \text{m fibre length}} \\
\sigma_{specific} &= T \times \rho \quad (\text{to convert cN/tex} \rightarrow \text{GPa}) \\
\sigma\ (\text{GPa}) &= T \times \rho \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** For a Nylon 6 fibre, T ≈ 8 cN/tex, ρ ≈ 1140 kg/m³, giving σ ≈ 0.91 GPa. For Kevlar-49, T ≈ 195 cN/tex, ρ ≈ 1440 kg/m³, giving σ ≈ 2.8 GPa.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Lab Problem)

**Problem:** A polyester film specimen of width 10 mm and thickness 0.05 mm is tested in tension. The gauge length is 50 mm. The specimen fails at a load of 78.5 N, with elongation at break of 220%. The initial linear region passes through (F = 5 N, ΔL = 0.25 mm). Determine: (i) Tensile strength, (ii) Elongation at break, (iii) Young's modulus, (iv) True stress at break.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= 10\ \text{mm} \times 0.05\ \text{mm} = 0.5\ \text{mm}^2 \\
\sigma_b &= \frac{F_b}{A_0} = \frac{78.5\ \text{N}}{0.5\ \text{mm}^2} = 157\ \text{MPa} \\
\varepsilon_b &= 220\% = 2.20 \\
E &= \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L} = \frac{5 \times 50}{0.5 \times 0.25} = \frac{250}{0.125} = 2000\ \text{MPa} = 2.0\ \text{GPa} \\
\sigma_{true,b} &= \sigma_b (1 + \varepsilon_b) = 157 \times 3.20 = 502.4\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix — Class of Material Recognition from Tensile Data**
>
> | Observation | Likely Material Class |
> |---|---|
> | σ_b < 50 MPa, ε_b > 200% | Elastomeric film (rubber, TPU) |
> | σ_b ≈ 150–250 MPa, ε_b ≈ 200–400% | PET / BOPET film |
> | σ_b ≈ 30–80 MPa, ε_b ≈ 10–30% | Cotton / wool textile |
> | σ_b (tenacity) ≈ 20–50 cN/tex, ε_b ≈ 15–25% | Polyester filament yarn |
> | σ_b (tenacity) ≈ 150–200 cN/tex, ε_b ≈ 3–5% | Aramid (Kevlar) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters. No markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels. Block-level functional architecture is used in place of physical drawings.

## 4.1 Universal Tensile Testing Architecture for Polymeric Materials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Specimen Prep] --> A2[Dimension Measurement]
    A2 --> A3[Grip Mounting]
    A3 --> B1[Load Cell]
    B1 --> B2[Crosshead Drive]
    B2 --> B3[Extensometer]
    B3 --> C1[Data Acquisition]
    C1 --> C2[Force Elongation]
    C2 --> C3[Stress Strain Conversion]
    C3 --> D1{Modulus E}
    C3 --> D2{Yield Stress}
    C3 --> D3{Break Point}
    D1 --> E1[Final Report]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    B2 -.feedback.-> A3
    C1 -.feedback.-> B2
```

**Interpretation of the architecture:**
- `Specimen Prep` (films cut with die, textiles with template, fibres mounted on paper tabs).
- `Load Cell` and `Crosshead Drive` operate in closed-loop via the data acquisition system.
- The `Stress Strain Conversion` node applies σ = F/A₀ and ε = ΔL/L₀ with stored specimen dimensions.

## 4.2 Class-Specific Tensile Response Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Polymeric Specimen] --> Q{Material Form}
    Q -->|Film| R1[Film Tensile]
    Q -->|Fabric Strip| R2[Textile Tensile]
    Q -->|Single Filament| R3[Fibre Tensile]
    R1 --> S1[ASTM D882 ISO 527 3]
    R2 --> S2[ASTM D5035 ISO 13934 1]
    R3 --> S3[ASTM D3822]
    S1 --> T1[Report MPa and percent]
    S2 --> T2[Report N and percent]
    S3 --> T3[Report cN per tex and percent]
    T1 --> U[Stress Strain Curve Plot]
    T2 --> U
    T3 --> U
    U --> V[Compute Modulus Toughness]
```

## 4.3 Typical Stress-Strain Curve Schematic (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin] --> A[Linear Elastic]
    A --> B[Yield Point]
    B --> C[Necking Cold Draw]
    C --> D[Strain Hardening]
    D --> E[Break Point]
    A -.Chord Modulus.- O
    B -.Peak Stress.-> A
    D -.Toughness Integral.-> O
    E -.Breaking Stress Strain.- O
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reading Guide:**
> - For a **film** (PET, LDPE), the curve passes through O → A → B → C → D → E.
> - For a **textile**, the curve is O → (concave-up A') → (linear B') → E (no yield point).
> - For a **fibre**, the curve is steep O → A → E (essentially linear, no plastic plateau).

## 4.4 Gripping and Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Sample Mounted] --> GripCheck{Valid Grip}
    GripCheck -->|Jaw Break| F1[Invalid: Reduce Grip Pressure]
    GripCheck -->|Edge Slip| F2[Invalid: Use Emery Paper or Pneumatic Grips]
    GripCheck -->|Gauge Mid-Section Break| F3[Valid Test: Record Curve]
    F1 --> GripCheck
    F2 --> GripCheck
    F3 --> Report[Compute Properties and Plot]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define (i) Tensile strength, (ii) Elongation at break, and (iii) Young's modulus of a polymeric membrane.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Tensile Strength (σᵦ or σₘₐₓ):** The maximum tensile stress a specimen sustains before fracture, calculated as the breaking load divided by the original cross-sectional area. Expressed in **MPa**.

(ii) **Elongation at Break (εᵦ):** The percentage increase in gauge length of a specimen at the moment of fracture, calculated as  
$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\ \%$$
A key indicator of film ductility.

(iii) **Young's Modulus (E):** The slope of the initial linear portion of the stress–strain curve, representing the intrinsic stiffness of the polymer. Calculated as E = σ/ε, in units of **MPa or GPa**.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between tenacity and tensile strength in the context of fibre testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Tensile Strength | Tenacity |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Maximum stress (force/original area) | Force at break per unit linear density |
| Units | MPa or GPa | cN/tex, gf/denier |
| Used for | Films, sheets, bulk polymers | Fibres, filaments, yarns |
| Area measurement | Required (A₀) | Not required (uses tex) |

> **Justification:** Tenacity is preferred for fibres because direct measurement of a filament's cross-sectional area is impractical and prone to error; linear density (mass per length) is far easier to determine.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a labeled stress–strain curve, describe the tensile behaviour of a **semi-crystalline polymeric film** (e.g., PET) up to fracture. Identify and justify the four key regions of the curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cotton textile strip of dimensions 50 mm × 200 mm (gauge length = 100 mm) is tested per ASTM D5035. The maximum load recorded is 920 N at an elongation of 14 mm. Compute (i) the breaking stress in N/cm width, (ii) the elongation at break in %, and (iii) the engineering strain at maximum load. Justify why textile strengths are conventionally reported in N/cm and not in MPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stress–Strain Regions of a Semi-Crystalline Film (PET):**

1. **Region I — Initial Elastic Region (O to A):**  
   Stress rises linearly with strain. Deformation is due to reversible bond stretching in amorphous regions. Slope = Young's Modulus E ≈ 2.5–4 GPa for PET.

2. **Region II — Yielding (A to B):**  
   The yield point is reached where shear bands initiate in the lamellae. Crazing may co-exist. Peak stress is the **yield stress σᵧ**.

3. **Region III — Cold Drawing / Necking (B to C):**  
   A localized neck forms and propagates along the gauge length. Chain segments align along the draw direction. Stress is roughly constant (drawing plateau).

4. **Region IV — Strain Hardening and Fracture (C to D → E):**  
   Once neck spans the entire gauge, molecular orientation stiffens the film, causing σ to rise sharply. Failure occurs at the break point (σᵦ, εᵦ).

> **Justification:** The four regions reflect the sequence of micro-mechanisms — bond stretching, lamellar shear, chain alignment, and fibrillar failure — unique to semi-crystalline polymers.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Breaking stress in N/cm} &= \frac{F_{max}}{\text{width}} = \frac{920\ \text{N}}{5.0\ \text{cm}} = 184\ \text{N/cm} \\
\text{(ii) Elongation at break } \varepsilon_b &= \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{14}{100} \times 100 = 14\% \\
\text{(iii) Engineering strain at max load} &= \frac{14}{100} = 0.14\ \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Justification for N/cm:** The thickness of a textile fabric is highly non-uniform and compressible, making A₀ unreliable. Strip tensile strength is therefore normalized by width (a measurable, reproducible geometric quantity), giving the conventional N/cm or N/50 mm unit.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **tenacity** and **initial modulus** as applied to a single-fibre tensile test. How are these quantities obtained from a force–elongation curve, and why are they preferred over engineering stress–strain measures for fibre characterization? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Nylon 6 filament of 84 denier (linear density = 9.33 tex) is tested. The fibre breaks at a load of 38 cN. The initial linear portion shows: F = 1.2 cN at ΔL/L₀ = 0.005, and F = 2.8 cN at ΔL/L₀ = 0.012. Compute: (i) tenacity in cN/tex, (ii) tenacity in gf/denier, (iii) initial modulus in cN/tex per %, and (iv) approximate specific stress in GPa, given ρ = 1140 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Tenacity and Initial Modulus for Fibres:**

- **Tenacity (T):** Breaking force per unit linear density (tex or denier). It is the specific strength of a fibre, allowing comparison of fibres of different thicknesses.

- **Initial Modulus (Eᵢ):** The slope of the initial linear portion of the specific stress–strain (or force–tex–strain) curve, typically measured between ε = 0.5% and ε = 1.0%. Indicates the fibre's resistance to initial elastic deformation.

> **Why preferred:** Fibres are slender (typical diameter 10–30 µm), making direct A₀ measurement prone to large errors. Linear density (tex) is determined gravimetrically with high accuracy. Tenacity and modulus per tex thus offer reproducible, comparable metrics.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Tenacity } T &= \frac{F_b}{\text{tex}} = \frac{38\ \text{cN}}{9.33\ \text{tex}} = 4.07\ \text{cN/tex} \\
\text{(ii) Tenacity in gf/denier:}\quad 1\ \text{gf} &= 0.98\ \text{cN} \approx 0.98\ \text{cN}; \quad 1\ \text{tex} = 9\ \text{denier} \\
T &= 4.07\ \text{cN/tex} = \frac{4.07}{9 \times 0.98} = 0.46\ \text{gf/denier} \\
\text{(iii) Initial modulus:}\quad E_i &= \frac{\Delta F / \text{tex}}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \frac{(2.8 - 1.2)/9.33}{(0.012 - 0.005) \times 100} \\
&= \frac{1.6 / 9.33}{0.7} = \frac{0.1715}{0.7} \approx 0.245\ \text{cN/tex per \%} \\
\text{(iv) Specific stress:}\quad \sigma &= T \times \rho \times 10^{-4} = 4.07 \times 1140 \times 10^{-4} = 0.464\ \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The Nylon 6 fibre has a tenacity of 4.07 cN/tex (moderate), initial modulus of 0.245 cN/tex/%, and a specific strength of ≈ 0.46 GPa — typical for apparel-grade Nylon.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Reporting tenacity in cN/tex without specifying the linear density in tex, OR reporting strength in MPa for textile strips. Both lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing engineering and true stress:** For large-strain films (ε_b > 1), the difference is significant (>50%). Examiners expect explicit formula usage.
> 3. **Reporting only breaking force, not stress:** For a 14-mark problem, omitting σ = F/A₀ is treated as incomplete.
> 4. **Not drawing or labeling the stress–strain curve:** A neat curve with named regions often carries 2–3 marks by itself.
> 5. **Grip failures ignored:** Stating "the sample slipped" without suggesting a remedy (emery paper, pneumatic grips) is a frequent deduction point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullets)**

- [x] **Tensile behaviour** is the σ–ε response of a material under uniaxial pull; for polymers, it is **rate- and temperature-dependent**.
- [x] **Key properties to extract:** Young's modulus (E), yield stress (σᵧ), tensile strength (σᵦ), elongation at break (εᵦ), and toughness (Uₜ).
- [x] **Three material classes** behave differently:
  - **Films/Membranes** → yield, cold-draw, strain-harden.
  - **Textiles** → J-shaped curve; yarn uncrimping dominates.
  - **Fibres** → linear up to break; high tenacity, low elongation (except elastomers).
- [x] **Standard formulas (must memorize):**
  - $\sigma = F/A_0$,  $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$
  - $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (in linear region)
  - $\sigma_{true} = \sigma (1+\varepsilon)$
  - $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$
  - $T = F_b / \text{tex}$
  - $U_t = \displaystyle\int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$
- [x] **Units to remember:** MPa, GPa, N/cm (textiles), cN/tex, gf/denier (fibres), %, mJ/m³ (toughness).
- [x] **Standards:** ASTM D882 (films), ASTM D5035 (textile strip), ASTM D3822 (fibre), ISO 13934-1, ISO 527-3.
- [x] **Sources of error in lab:** grip slippage, jaw break, specimen misalignment, variable thickness, environmental humidity (especially for natural fibres like cotton/jute).
- [x] **For textile/fibre data:** report force vs. elongation, NOT stress, if the cross-section is uncertain.
- [x] **For lab report writing:** Always include the stress–strain plot, a tabulated raw data set, and a discussion of the failure mode.
- [x] **Toughness ≠ Strength:** A tough material has high area under curve; a strong material has high σᵦ.
