- [x] **Engineering vs. true stress:** Use engineering for design (initial geometry); use true for understanding material flow (necking region).
- [x] **High-modulus fibres (Kevlar, carbon, UHMWPE)** show low elongation, high tenacity, **low toughness in energy terms** despite high strength.
- [x] **Rubbery films** show very high εᵦ (>500%) but low σᵦ and low E — typical hyperelastic response (not Hookean).
- [x] **Effect of strain rate:** Higher crosshead speed → higher σᵧ, lower εᵦ for most polymers (viscoelastic stiffening).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Image Correlation techniques for the study of material behaviour under various loading conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL307 - PCCVL307
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Image Correlation techniques for the study of material behaviour under various loading conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Image Correlation (DIC)** is a non-contact, full-field, optical-mechanical measurement technique that quantitatively evaluates the in-plane displacement and strain fields on the surface of a specimen by tracking the grey-level intensity patterns of random speckle patterns (or natural surface textures) in a series of digital images captured before, during, and after the application of mechanical, thermal, or environmental loading.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCVL307 – Experimental Mechanics / Structural Health Monitoring laboratory module)**, DIC is officially classified as an **advanced, image-based, photogrammetric experimental method** that belongs to the family of **Computer Vision Assisted Experimental Stress Analysis (CVAESA)** techniques. It forms part of the modernized experimental toolkit alongside strain gauges, extensometers, and Moiré interferometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** DIC is to be understood as an *experimental procedure* (Module Experiment 2) — not merely a software tool. Students must demonstrate understanding of the *optical setup*, *mathematical correlation principle*, *speckle preparation*, and *interpretation of strain maps* for materials under tensile, compressive, flexural, fatigue, and fracture loading.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Fingerprint Matching for Materials"

Imagine you place a polka-dot patterned sticker on a rubber sheet and stretch it. The dots move with the material. If you take a photo *before* stretching and another *after*, then ask a computer: **"Find the new location of every tiny group of dots from the first photo in the second photo"** — you get a complete map of how the surface moved and deformed. That is **Digital Image Correlation**.

- The **polka-dot pattern** = the *speckle pattern* applied to the specimen.
- The **group of dots** = a *subset* (the basic unit of tracking).
- The **search for the new position** = *cross-correlation mathematics*.
- The **map of how the sheet moved** = the *full-field displacement and strain field*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in DIC:**
> - Pixel resolution: typically **1–10 µm/pixel** for laboratory DIC.
> - Spatial resolution: **subset size** (commonly 21×21 to 41×41 pixels).
> - Strain accuracy: **~10–100 microstrain (µε)** under optimal conditions.
> - Camera: **CCD/CMOS** with ≥ **5 MP** resolution recommended.
> - Light source: **blue LED (~460 nm)** to maximize speckle contrast.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Evolution

| Era | Technique | Key Limitation |
|------|------------|---------------|
| Pre-1980 | Moiré, Photoelasticity | Requires gratings; limited strain range |
| **1980s** | **DIC pioneers (Peters, Ranson, Sutton)** | Coarse resolution; slow computers |
| 2000s | 2D-DIC with high-speed cameras | Loss of out-of-plane info |
| **2010–Present** | **Stereo (3D) DIC, High-Temperature DIC, Real-time DIC** | **Current KTU lab standard** |

The modern DIC technique was crystallized by **Sutton, Orteu, and Schreier (2009)** in their seminal text *"Image Correlation for Shape, Motion and Deformation Measurements"*, which remains the global reference text for graduate-level experimental mechanics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The DIC technique operates in **five sequential stages**:

### Stage 1 — Specimen Preparation
A **random high-contrast speckle pattern** is applied to the specimen surface. Common methods:
- **Spray paint (matte black on white basecoat)** — most common in KTU labs.
- **Airbrush speckling** — for fine-grained research work.
- **Photolithography / microprinting** — for MEMS-scale specimens.
- **Natural texture** — for concrete, rock, wood (no pattern needed).

### Stage 2 — Image Acquisition
- A **reference image** (undeformed state) is captured: $I_{ref}(x, y)$.
- A sequence of **deformed images** $I_{def}(x', y')$ are captured at known load increments.
- **Lens distortions** and **perspective errors** must be calibrated (using a calibration grid).

### Stage 3 — Subset Definition & Matching
- The reference image is divided into small square regions called **subsets** $S$ (e.g., 31×31 pixels).
- For each subset centroid, a *search window* in the deformed image is defined.
- The algorithm finds the new position $(x', y')$ that *maximally matches* the grey-level intensity pattern.

### Stage 4 — Cross-Correlation Computation
The matching is performed using a **correlation criterion**. The two most common are:
- **Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC)** — robust to lighting changes.
- **Zero-Normalized Sum of Squared Differences (ZNSSD)** — robust to scale changes.

### Stage 5 — Displacement & Strain Field Extraction
Once every subset center is tracked, the **displacement field** $(u, v)$ is obtained. The **strain field** is then derived by **numerical differentiation** of the displacement field.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\sum f(x,y) \cdot g(x',y')}{\sqrt{\sum f^2 \cdot \sum g^2}}$ | Classical Cross-Correlation (CC) coefficient | Dimensionless; range $\vert C \vert \leq 1$ |
| 2 | $C_{ZNCC} = \dfrac{\sum (f - \bar{f})(g - \bar{g})}{\sqrt{\sum (f-\bar{f})^2 \sum (g-\bar{g})^2}}$ | Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (most used) | Insensitive to linear lighting shifts |
| 3 | $C_{ZNSSD} = \sum \left[ \dfrac{f - \bar{f}}{\sqrt{\sum (f-\bar{f})^2}} - \dfrac{g - \bar{g}}{\sqrt{\sum (g-\bar{g})^2}} \right]^2$ | Zero-Normalized SSD criterion | Minimize; used when scale varies |
| 4 | $x' = x + u + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\Delta x + \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\Delta y$ | First-order shape function (subset mapping) | Pixel units; $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are local coordinates |
| 5 | $y' = y + v + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\Delta x + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\Delta y$ | First-order shape function (v-component) | Captures translation + rotation + shear |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$ | Normal strain components | Dimensionless (or mm/mm) |
| 7 | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}$ | Engineering shear strain | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{1,2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_{xx} - \varepsilon_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$ | Principal strains | Critical for failure analysis |
| 9 | $L = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + f^2}$ | Stereo-DIC camera baseline equation | $f$ = focal length; $L$ = optical path |
| 10 | $P = \dfrac{N_p \cdot s}{M}$ | Measurement precision formula | $N_p$ = pixels; $s$ = pixel size; $M$ = magnification |

## 2.3 Why Each Step is Necessary — The "Why & How" Engineer's View

- **Why speckle pattern?** Without high-frequency spatial variation in intensity, the algorithm cannot *uniquely identify* a subset. A pure-white or pure-black surface gives **zero correlation contrast** — it has no features to track.
- **Why a subset (not a single pixel)?** A single pixel is ambiguous; many surface points have identical intensity. A 2D patch of pixels provides a *unique signature* — analogous to a fingerprint.
- **Why normalization (ZNCC)?** During loading, lighting may drift, exposure may change, and shading may vary. ZNCC removes these linear effects and reports *only structural matching*.
- **Why shape functions?** Pure translation is rare in real deformation; the subset also rotates, shears, and stretches. A first-order shape function adds 4 parameters (2 translations + 2 gradients) to model affine deformation of the subset.
- **Why strain via differentiation?** Displacement is the *primary measurement*; strain is the *derived engineering quantity* required for stress analysis via Hooke's law: $\sigma = E \varepsilon$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of DIC |
|-------------------|------------|
| **Civil / Structural** | Crack-tip strain mapping in concrete beams under flexure; bond-slip in RC joints |
| **Mechanical** | Formability limits in sheet metal; fatigue crack growth monitoring |
| **Aerospace** | Composite laminate inter-ply shear; high-temperature turbine blade creep |
| **Biomedical** | Soft tissue (tendon, skin) biomechanics; vascular wall motion |
| **Geotechnical** | Rock joint displacement; soil shear-band localization |
| **Forensic Engineering** | Post-failure deformation reconstruction of failed components |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DIC dominates modern labs:** It requires *no physical contact* (preserves the natural stress state), provides *continuous full-field data* (not point data), works on *arbitrary geometries*, and tolerates *very high strains* (>100%) — far beyond the 1–2% limit of foil strain gauges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC) Criterion

### Objective
To derive the mathematical form of the ZNCC coefficient that DIC software minimizes the *negative* of, in order to find the sub-pixel displacement between two image subsets.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Subset Intensity Fields
Let the reference subset be centered at integer pixel $(x_0, y_0)$ with intensity $f(x, y)$. After deformation, the corresponding subset in the deformed image is $g(x', y')$, where $x', y'$ are the *unknown* deformed coordinates.

$$f = f(x, y) \quad \text{with} \quad (x, y) \in S_{ref}$$

$$g = g(x', y') \quad \text{with} \quad (x', y') \in S_{def}$$

### Step 2 — Write the Raw (Non-Normalized) Cross-Correlation Coefficient
The classical cross-correlation coefficient is the *normalized inner product* of the two intensity fields:

$$C = \frac{\sum_{(x,y) \in S} f(x,y) \cdot g(x',y')}{\sqrt{\sum f^2(x,y) \cdot \sum g^2(x',y')}}$$

> **Logic:** Maximizing the inner product maximizes the *alignment* of bright and dark features; the denominator makes it independent of overall brightness scale.

### Step 3 — Introduce the Mean Intensity Correction
Because lighting may add a *constant offset* (additive) or multiply intensities (multiplicative), we subtract the local mean from each subset. Define:

$$\bar{f} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{(x,y) \in S} f(x,y)$$

$$\bar{g} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{(x',y') \in S} g(x',y')$$

where $N$ is the number of pixels in the subset.

### Step 4 — Write the Mean-Centered Intensities
Replace $f \to f - \bar{f}$ and $g \to g - \bar{g}$. The cross-correlation becomes:

$$C = \frac{\sum (f - \bar{f})(g - \bar{g})}{\sqrt{\sum (f - \bar{f})^2 \cdot \sum (g - \bar{g})^2}}$$

> **Logic:** Subtracting the mean removes the *additive DC component* (ambient lighting baseline), so only the *local contrast patterns* contribute to matching.

### Step 5 — Normalize by the Local Energy (Standard Deviation)
The denominator of the previous equation is the product of the *standard deviations* (up to a factor of $N$) of the two subsets. Dividing by this product forces $C$ into the bounded range $\vert C \vert \leq 1$:

$$C_{ZNCC}(u, v) = \frac{\sum_{(x,y) \in S} [f(x,y) - \bar{f}] \cdot [g(x+u, y+v) - \bar{g}]}{\sqrt{\sum [f(x,y) - \bar{f}]^2 \cdot \sum [g(x+u, y+v) - \bar{g}]^2}}$$

### Step 6 — Final Statement of the Optimization Problem
The DIC algorithm seeks the integer shift $(u, v)$ that maximizes $C_{ZNCC}$. The *sub-pixel refinement* then uses a parabolic/Gaussian fit around the integer peak.

$$\boxed{\;(u^{*}, v^{*}) = \arg\max_{(u, v)} \; C_{ZNCC}(u, v)\;}$$

> **Why sub-pixel refinement matters:** A pure integer-pixel search yields 1-pixel accuracy, i.e., ~10 µm. Parabolic interpolation around the peak gives ~0.01-pixel accuracy, i.e., **~100 nm** — a 100× improvement, critical for strain calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Engineering Strain from the Displacement Field

### Step 1 — Define the Cauchy Infinitesimal Strain Tensor
For small deformations, the normal strain in the $x$-direction is the *gradient of horizontal displacement*:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u(x, y)}{\partial x}$$

> **Logic:** Strain = relative change in length. If two points separated by $dx$ move apart by $du$, the strain is $du / dx$.

### Step 2 — Derive the Numerical (Finite Difference) Form
DIC gives discrete $u$ values at pixel centers. We approximate the gradient by a **central difference** (second-order accurate):

$$\varepsilon_{xx}(x_i, y_j) = \frac{u(x_{i+1}, y_j) - u(x_{i-1}, y_j)}{2 \, \Delta x}$$

where $\Delta x$ is the calibrated physical spacing between adjacent data points (in mm), and $i, j$ are integer grid indices.

### Step 3 — Write All Three In-Plane Strain Components
By the same logic applied to $v$ and to cross-derivatives:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

### Step 4 — Derive Principal Strains (for Failure Prediction)
The in-plane strain tensor in matrix form is:

$$[\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} & \gamma_{xy}/2 \\ \gamma_{xy}/2 & \varepsilon_{yy} \end{bmatrix}$$

The eigenvalues of this tensor are the **principal strains**:

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_{xx} - \varepsilon_{yy}}{2}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

> **Logic:** Principal strains are the *maximum and minimum normal strains* experienced by the material element — they are invariant to the coordinate system and govern failure initiation in brittle and ductile materials alike.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — "Tensile Coupon under Uniaxial Load"

**Problem:** A flat mild-steel coupon (gauge length 50 mm, width 12 mm) is loaded in tension to 18 kN. A 2D-DIC system with calibration giving **1 pixel = 0.05 mm** records the following displacements at two adjacent measurement points along the loading axis:

| Point | $u$ (pixels) | $v$ (pixels) |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| A $(x_A, y_A)$ | 12.34 | 0.21 |
| B $(x_B, y_A)$ | 14.10 | 0.19 |
| C $(x_A, y_C)$ | 12.30 | 0.45 |

Distance $AB = 20$ pixels (i.e., 1.0 mm), $AC = 18$ pixels (i.e., 0.9 mm).

**Find:** $\varepsilon_{xx}$, $\varepsilon_{yy}$, $\gamma_{xy}$ at point A.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert pixel displacements to physical units**

$$u_A = 12.34 \times 0.05 = 0.6170 \;\text{mm}, \quad v_A = 0.21 \times 0.05 = 0.0105 \;\text{mm}$$

$$u_B = 14.10 \times 0.05 = 0.7050 \;\text{mm}, \quad v_B = 0.19 \times 0.05 = 0.0095 \;\text{mm}$$

$$u_C = 12.30 \times 0.05 = 0.6150 \;\text{mm}, \quad v_C = 0.45 \times 0.05 = 0.0225 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\varepsilon_{xx}$ (longitudinal strain)**

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{u_B - u_A}{\Delta x_{AB}} = \frac{0.7050 - 0.6170}{1.0} = 0.0880 \;\text{(i.e., 8.80%)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\varepsilon_{yy}$ (transverse strain, using Poisson effect)**

$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{v_C - v_A}{\Delta y_{AC}} = \frac{0.0225 - 0.0105}{0.9} = 0.0133 \;\text{(i.e., 1.33%)}$$

> **Note:** Strain is positive in both directions — we are in the *plastic regime* of mild steel (Poisson's ratio here ≈ 0.15, lower than the elastic value 0.30, confirming plastic flow).

**Step 4 — Compute $\gamma_{xy}$ (shear strain)**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{u_C - u_A}{\Delta y} = \frac{0.6150 - 0.6170}{0.9} = -0.00222$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{v_B - v_A}{\Delta x} = \frac{0.0095 - 0.0105}{1.0} = -0.00100$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = -0.00222 + (-0.00100) = -0.00322 \;\text{(i.e., -0.322%)}$$

**Step 5 — Verification by Volumetric Strain**

$$\varepsilon_{vol} = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} = 0.0880 + 0.0133 = 0.1013$$

> Positive volumetric strain → material is *expanding* (typical of ductile metals in the post-yield regime, due to void nucleation and growth).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — End-to-End DIC Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Preparation: Apply Speckle Pattern] --> B[Camera + Lens Calibration: Use Calibration Grid]
    B --> C[Capture Reference Image: Unloaded State]
    C --> D[Apply Incremental Load via UTM/MTS]
    D --> E[Capture Deformed Image Sequence]
    E --> F[Define Subset Size and Step Size]
    F --> G[Sub-pixel Search via ZNCC Maximization]
    G --> H{Is Correlation Coefficient > 0.95?}
    H -- No --> I[Refine Shape Function: Add 2nd Order]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Generate Raw Displacement Field ux, vy]
    J --> K[Numerical Differentiation of Displacement Field]
    K --> L[Compute Strain Tensors exx, eyy, gxy]
    L --> M[Compute Principal Strains e1, e2]
    M --> N[Visualization: Contour Maps and Vector Plots]
    N --> O[Engineering Interpretation: Failure Zones, Crack Tips]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 2D-DIC vs Stereo 3D-DIC Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["2D-DIC Setup: Single Camera"]
        S1A[Specimen with Speckle] --> S1B[Single CCD Camera]
        S1B --> S1C[2D In-Plane Displacement Field]
    end
    subgraph S2["Stereo 3D-DIC Setup: Twin Cameras"]
        S2A[Specimen with Speckle] --> S2B[Left Camera]
        S2A --> S2C[Right Camera]
        S2B --> S2D[Triangulation Algorithm]
        S2C --> S2D
        S2D --> S2E[3D Full-Field Displacement and Strain]
    end
    S1 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph opt["Optical Subsystem"]
        L1[White/Blue LED Light Source]
        L2[Polarizing Filter: Reduce Specular Reflection]
        L3[Telecentric Lens: Eliminate Perspective Error]
    end
    subgraph cam["Image Acquisition Subsystem"]
        C1[High-Resolution CMOS Camera: >= 5 MP]
        C2[Frame Grabber Card or USB 3.0 Interface]
        C3[Synchronized Trigger Unit for Dynamic Tests]
    end
    subgraph proc["Computational Subsystem"]
        P1[Reference Image: Iref]
        P2[Deformed Image: Idef]
        P3[Subset Correlation Engine: ZNCC]
        P4[Sub-pixel Interpolation: Parabolic or Gaussian]
        P5[Displacement Field: u, v]
        P6[Strain Field: exx, eyy, gxy]
    end
    opt --> cam
    cam --> P1
    cam --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Digital Image Correlation (DIC). List any four advantages of DIC over conventional strain gauge measurement.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is a non-contact, full-field optical measurement technique that tracks the movement of random speckle patterns on a specimen surface to compute in-plane displacement and strain fields during mechanical loading.

**Advantages (4 × 0.375 marks = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Non-contact** — does not disturb the natural stress state of the specimen.
2. **Full-field data** — measures displacement at thousands of points simultaneously, unlike a strain gauge which gives point data only.
3. **No specimen preparation** for natural-texture materials; arbitrary geometry accommodated.
4. **High strain tolerance** — can measure strains >100%, far beyond the 1–2% limit of foil gauges.
5. *(Optional 5th)*: Works under harsh environments (high temperature, underwater, vacuum).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is a speckle pattern, and why is it essential for DIC? List the two most common speckle preparation techniques.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Speckle pattern (1 mark):** A speckle pattern is a *high-contrast, random distribution of fine black (or dark) dots on a white (or light) background* applied to the specimen surface. It serves as the *fiducial marker grid* that the DIC algorithm tracks from image to image.

**Why essential (1 mark):** Without spatial variation in intensity, the algorithm cannot uniquely identify sub-regions. The speckle provides the *high-frequency content* necessary for unique subset matching.

**Two common techniques (1 mark):**
1. **Spray painting** — matte white basecoat + fine black mist spray (most common in labs).
2. **Airbrush speckling** — for finer, more uniform patterns in research work.
3. *(Optional)*: **Photolithography** for MEMS specimens; **powder dusting** for soft materials.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working principle of 2D-DIC with a labeled schematic. Describe the role of the subset, shape function, and correlation criterion in the measurement process.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** 2D-DIC measures in-plane displacements by comparing a reference (undeformed) digital image to a sequence of deformed images. The technique was pioneered by Peters, Ranson, Sutton, and others in the 1980s.

**Working Principle – Stepwise (4 marks):**

1. **Image Acquisition:** A digital camera captures a reference image $I_{ref}(x, y)$ of the speckled specimen. A deformed image $I_{def}(x', y')$ is captured after load application.
2. **Subset Selection:** The reference image is divided into small square *subsets* of size $N \times N$ pixels (typically $N = 21$ to $41$). Each subset is identified by its centroid coordinates $(x_c, y_c)$.
3. **Shape Function Mapping:** The deformed position of each point inside the subset is modeled as:
   $$x' = x_0 + u + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\Delta y, \quad y' = y_0 + v + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\Delta y$$
   This first-order shape function captures translation, rotation, shear, and uniform stretch of the subset.
4. **Correlation Search:** For each subset, a *search window* in the deformed image is scanned. The ZNCC coefficient is computed at every candidate integer pixel shift $(u, v)$:
   $$C_{ZNCC}(u, v) = \frac{\sum [f(x,y) - \bar{f}][g(x+u, y+v) - \bar{g}]}{\sqrt{\sum [f - \bar{f}]^2 \sum [g - \bar{g}]^2}}$$
5. **Sub-pixel Refinement:** A 2D Gaussian or parabolic surface is fitted through the integer-pixel peak and its neighbors, giving sub-pixel displacement precision (~0.01 pixel).

**Schematic (1 mark):** Refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.2 (Single Camera path). The diagram should show: Speckled specimen → Camera → Image storage → Correlation engine → Displacement/strain output.

**Role Summary (1 mark):**
- *Subset* = the basic unit of tracking (analogous to a fingerprint region).
- *Shape function* = the geometric model that maps how the subset has deformed.
- *Correlation criterion* (ZNCC) = the mathematical similarity measure that locates the new subset position.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A steel coupon is tested under tension. The 2D-DIC system records the following displacement data at three neighboring points on the specimen surface. Compute $\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}, \gamma_{xy}$ and the principal strains at point A. Use the calibration: 1 pixel = 0.04 mm.

| Point | $x$ (px) | $y$ (px) | $u$ (px) | $v$ (px) |
|------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| A | 100 | 100 | 10.00 | 0.50 |
| B | 130 | 100 | 13.20 | 0.46 |
| C | 100 | 125 | 9.95 | 1.10 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physical displacements (1 mark):**
- $u_A = 10.00 \times 0.04 = 0.4000$ mm; $v_A = 0.50 \times 0.04 = 0.0200$ mm
- $u_B = 13.20 \times 0.04 = 0.5280$ mm; $v_B = 0.46 \times 0.04 = 0.0184$ mm
- $u_C = 9.95 \times 0.04 = 0.3980$ mm; $v_C = 1.10 \times 0.04 = 0.0440$ mm

**Step 2 — Physical distances (1 mark):**
- $\Delta x_{AB} = (130 - 100) \times 0.04 = 1.20$ mm
- $\Delta y_{AC} = (125 - 100) \times 0.04 = 1.00$ mm

**Step 3 — Compute strains (2 marks):**
$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{u_B - u_A}{\Delta x} = \frac{0.5280 - 0.4000}{1.20} = \frac{0.1280}{1.20} = 0.1067 = 10.67\%$$

$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{v_C - v_A}{\Delta y} = \frac{0.0440 - 0.0200}{1.00} = 0.0240 = 2.40\%$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{u_C - u_A}{\Delta y} = \frac{0.3980 - 0.4000}{1.00} = -0.0020$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{v_B - v_A}{\Delta x} = \frac{0.0184 - 0.0200}{1.20} = -0.00133$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = -0.0020 + (-0.00133) = -0.00333 = -0.333\%$$

**Step 4 — Principal strains (2 marks):**
$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{0.1067 + 0.0240}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{0.1067 - 0.0240}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{-0.00333}{2}\right)^2}$$

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = 0.06535 \pm \sqrt{(0.04135)^2 + (-0.001665)^2}$$

$$\varepsilon_{1,2} = 0.06535 \pm \sqrt{0.001710 + 0.0000028} = 0.06535 \pm 0.04138$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_1 = 0.1067 = 10.67\%, \quad \varepsilon_2 = 0.02397 = 2.40\%}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The material has entered the *plastic regime*; principal strain $\varepsilon_1 \approx 10.7\%$ is well above the elastic limit (~0.2%) for mild steel. Poisson's ratio in plastic flow is $\nu_p = -\varepsilon_{yy}/\varepsilon_{xx} = -0.024/0.1067 = -0.225$ — this is positive, confirming the tensile plastic flow (volumetric expansion due to void formation).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Differentiate between 2D-DIC and Stereo 3D-DIC. List the situations in which 3D-DIC is mandatory and explain the underlying optical principle that makes it possible.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | 2D-DIC | Stereo 3D-DIC |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| Cameras | Single | Two (or more) |
| Measures | In-plane only $(u, v)$ | In-plane + out-of-plane $(u, v, w)$ |
| Surface constraint | Flat, in-plane only | Arbitrary curved 3D surfaces |
| Out-of-plane displacement | Treated as error | Quantified |
| Calibration | Simple scale factor | Full camera calibration (intrinsics + extrinsics) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Typical use | Lab tensile tests | Curved shells, buckling, impact |

**Optical Principle (3 marks):** Stereo DIC uses the **principle of triangulation**, identical to human stereo vision. Two cameras separated by a known baseline $B$ view the same surface point from two angles. The 3D coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ of every point are reconstructed from the **disparity** (difference in pixel positions) between the two camera views. The governing relation is:

$$Z = \frac{B \cdot f}{d + \Delta d}$$

where $f$ is the focal length, $d$ is the disparity, and $\Delta d$ is the disparity correction from calibration. As the specimen deforms, changes in $X, Y, Z$ at each subset yield all three displacement components.

**When 3D-DIC is mandatory (1 mark):**
1. **Curved specimens** (pipes, shells, biomechanical tissues).
2. **Buckling tests** where out-of-plane deflection is significant.
3. **Impact / high-rate events** with 3D rigid-body motion.
4. **Large rotations** of the specimen surface during loading.
5. **Multi-axial stress states** on non-planar geometries.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the experimental procedure to perform a DIC test on a concrete beam under three-point bending, including specimen preparation, optical setup, loading steps, and interpretation of the strain contour map to identify the crack initiation zone.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation (1.5 marks):**
- Cast a concrete beam of size, e.g., $100 \times 100 \times 500$ mm.
- After 28 days of curing, clean the tension face (soffit) of the beam.
- Apply a **matte white basecoat** (typically water-based acrylic paint).
- After drying, apply a **fine mist of black spray paint** to produce a *random, isotropic, high-contrast speckle pattern*.
- Verify: speckle size should be **3–5 pixels** in the camera image; speckle density should be ~50% coverage.

**Step 2 — Optical Setup (1.5 marks):**
- Mount the beam on a **three-point bending fixture** (simply supported, central load).
- Place a **5-MP CMOS camera** with a **telecentric lens** (or a standard lens with calibration) on a tripod, perpendicular to the speckled face.
- Distance chosen such that 1 pixel ≈ 0.05–0.10 mm.
- Install **two LED light sources** at 45° angles to eliminate specular reflections.
- Mount a **calibration grid** (e.g., 12×9 dot grid, 10 mm spacing) in front of the specimen; capture calibration images from the same camera position.
- For 3D-DIC, use *two cameras* in a stereo configuration with a baseline of ~150 mm.

**Step 3 — Loading and Image Capture (1.5 marks):**
- Capture a **reference image** of the unloaded beam.
- Apply load in **displacement-controlled mode** (e.g., 0.05 mm/min) on a UTM.
- Synchronize image capture with load steps: capture every 1 kN increment (or every 0.1 mm displacement).
- Record load $P$ and crosshead displacement $\delta$ for each image.

**Step 4 — DIC Post-Processing (1.5 marks):**
- Import reference and deformed images into DIC software (e.g., GOM Correlate, VIC-2D, Ncorr, OpenDIC).
- Define Region of Interest (ROI) covering the central gauge length.
- Set subset size = 31 pixels, step size = 5 pixels, criterion = ZNCC.
- Run correlation; export displacement fields $(u, v)$ and compute strain field.

**Step 5 — Interpretation of Strain Contour (1 mark):**
- Plot the **principal tensile strain** $\varepsilon_1$ as a color contour map.
- **Crack initiation zone identification:** regions where $\varepsilon_1$ exceeds the **ultimate tensile strain of concrete** (~100–150 µε for normal strength concrete) are flagged.
- A **localized band of high strain** perpendicular to the maximum principal stress direction indicates impending flexural crack.
- The **crack tip strain field** shows characteristic $1/\sqrt{r}$ singularity (linear elastic fracture mechanics) — measurable directly by DIC, impossible with strain gauges.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mean intensity correction** in ZNCC: -1 to -2 marks.
> 2. **Using integer-pixel search only** (no sub-pixel interpolation) and claiming "DIC accuracy is 1 pixel" — wrong; always mention *parabolic/Gaussian sub-pixel refinement* for full marks.
> 3. **Confusing 2D-DIC with stereo 3D-DIC** in curved surface questions — read the question carefully.
> 4. **Sign convention error** in $\gamma_{xy}$: it is the *sum* of the two cross-derivatives, not the average.
> 5. **Skipping the speckle preparation step** in procedure questions — examiners explicitly test this.
> 6. **Confusing normal strain with shear strain** definitions.
> 7. **Not mentioning calibration** — every KTU procedure question expects "calibration grid used to obtain pixel-to-mm scale factor."
> 8. **Volumetric strain sign**: students often forget that plastic flow in ductile metals causes *expansion* (positive volumetric strain).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Recall Summary (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024):**

- **DIC is a non-contact, full-field, image-based** displacement and strain measurement technique.
- **Three essential ingredients**: (i) random high-contrast speckle, (ii) high-resolution digital camera, (iii) cross-correlation algorithm.
- **Subset size** is the basic tracking unit; typical range 21–41 pixels.
- **ZNCC criterion** is the most widely used correlation measure; insensitive to linear lighting variations.
- **First-order shape function** adds 4 deformation parameters (2 translations + 2 gradients) to model affine subset deformation.
- **Sub-pixel refinement** via parabolic/Gaussian fit is mandatory for strain-quality accuracy (~0.01 pixel = ~100 nm precision).
- **Strain = numerical gradient of displacement**; use central differences for second-order accuracy.
- **2D-DIC** measures in-plane $(u, v)$ only; **Stereo 3D-DIC** adds out-of-plane $(w)$ via triangulation.
- **Calibration grid** is mandatory to convert pixels → mm; $1$ pixel $= s/M$ where $s$ = sensor pixel size, $M$ = magnification.
- **Principal strains** $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$ are eigenvalues of the in-plane strain tensor and govern failure initiation.
- **Engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{xy} = \partial u / \partial y + \partial v / \partial x$ (NOT the tensor shear $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$).
- **Speckle pattern quality** (random, isotropic, high contrast, 3–5 pixel feature size) is the *single biggest determinant* of DIC accuracy.
- **Applications across loading types**:
  - *Tensile* → Young's modulus, yield strain, Poisson's ratio, Lüders band propagation.
  - *Compressive* → concrete crushing zones, confined concrete dilation.
  - *Flexural* → crack-tip strain field, neutral axis migration, tension-stiffening in RC.
  - *Fatigue* → cyclic ratcheting, hysteresis loop extraction.
  - *Impact* → high-speed DIC at ≥100,000 fps; dynamic fracture energy.
  - *Thermal-mechanical* → coupled DIC + IR thermography.
- **Advantages over strain gauges**: full-field, no contact, very high strain range, no fatigue degradation of sensor.
- **Limitations**: requires line-of-sight, surface preparation needed (except natural texture), computationally intensive for high-resolution fields, sensitive to vibration.
- **Volumetric strain in plastic flow of metals is POSITIVE** (not zero) — due to void nucleation; this is a frequently-tested KTU viva question.
- **First-order vs second-order shape functions**: 1st-order handles affine deformation; 2nd-order adds 4 more terms to handle quadratic deformation at high strain gradients near crack tips.
